pages.text:
Historical Perspective
Before the mid-twentieth century, injury to the spinal cord was 
synonymous with death, either instantly or after a period of great 
suffering. The Edwin Smith papyrus, written almost 5000 years 
ago, contains descriptions of cases of cervical spinal cord injury 
(SCI), among other traumatic injuries, along with their recom -
mended treatments. The author noted the cervical SCI cases  
represent “an ailment not to be treated.”
77 During the early nine-
teenth century, Lord Nelson, admiral of the British fleet, received 
a gunshot wound to his chest during the Battle of T rafalgar. He 
was taken immediately to the ship’s surgeon to whom he noted, 
“All power of motion and feeling below my chest are gone.” The 
surgeon replied, “My lord, unhappily for our Country, nothing 
can be done for you.” Later in the century, US President James 
Garfield experienced a gunshot wound to the conus medullaris; 
he survived only 3 months.
87
During the early part of the twentieth century, the prognosis for 
surviving SCI remained poor. During World War I, 80% of Amer-
ican soldiers with SCI died within 2 weeks; the 20% surviving 
longer had incomplete injuries. The survival of British soldiers who 
sustained SCI at this time was better than that of the Americans. 
Nevertheless, an estimated 80% were also dead by 3 years. In con-
trast, however, German care for survivors of SCI was significantly 
better, with a reported mortality rate of only 33%.
75
Through the experience of seeing vast numbers of spinally in -
jured soldiers during World War I, pioneering clinicians gained 
insights in how persons with SCI could be treated differently than 
they had historically been treated or not treated, which had  
resulted in poor outcomes. Otfrid Foerster, a neurologist and self-
taught German neurosurgeon, treated nearly 5000 nervous  
system injuries during the war.
218 He advocated for physical train-
ing for restoration of function, pioneered interventions such as 
rhizotomies to treat severe spasticity and facilitate mobility, and 
was notably a mentor of Ludwig Guttmann before Guttmann’s 
escape from Nazi Germany. At the same time in the United States, 
Donald Munro developed an SCI unit in Boston, which became 
an early model of SCI care in the United States. There he also 
advocated for physical training and began to systematically ad -
dress some of the secondary complications of SCI that had his -
torically led to early mortality, including initiating a procedure of 
filling and draining the bladder to reduce infection and requiring 
frequent turning of patients in bed to prevent the development of 
pressure injuries.
75
By midcentury, Harry Botterell and Al Jousse, influenced by 
Munro’s example, had established the first SCI center in T oronto, 
Canada. At the same time in England, at the National Spinal In -
jury Centre at Stoke Mandeville Hospital, Guttmann pioneered 
an even more comprehensive approach to care that combined 
physical training, nutrition, and psychological aspects of SCI 
along with care of the skin, bladder, and bowel.
75,218 His approach 
evolved to become the foundation of the expertise of SCI physi-
cians today. The program he directed became the standard of care 
for SCI units and was adopted around the globe. Guttmann also 
• Complete SCIs do not exhibit any motor or sensory function  
at sacral levels S4–S5 or on digital rectal exam. Incomplete SCIs  
do have sensory and/or motor sparing at these levels.
• The amount of a physical/functional independence a person 
can achieve after SCI is dependent on the completeness of  
the injury and the single neurological level determined by the 
ISNCSCI.
• Autonomic dysreflexia is a potentially life-threatening complica-
tion of higher level SCIs, and recognizing it and knowing how 
to treat it are essential.
• The leading cause of death in persons with SCI is pulmonary/  
respiratory complications.
• Rehabilitation measures are critical to improve function,  
duration, and quality of life in patients with SCI.
50
Spinal Cord Injury
MIGUEL X. ESCALON, GEORGE MARZLOFF, AND THOMAS N. BRYCE
• Spinal cord injury (SCI) is caused by damage to upper or lower 
motor neurons that pass through or arise from the spinal cord 
and can present as an upper motor neuron syndrome, lower 
motor neuron syndrome, or a mixed picture depending on the 
level of the lesion.
• SCI can presen t with weakness, changes in sensation, and  
loss of control of bodily functions at and below the level of  
the lesion.
• Trauma is the most common cause of SCI in the United States, 
with motor vehicle accidents being the most common etiology 
of traumatic SCI.
• The International Standards for Neurological Classification of 
SCI (ISNCSCI) is a tool used to determine the level of SCI and 
whether the injury is complete or incomplete.
SUMMARY PEARLS

founded the first journal and the first professional society dedi -
cated to SCI research and care. He is often referred to as the father 
of SCI care because of these accomplishments. In the United 
States during World War II, special SCI units were developed in 
army hospitals (which later became Veterans Administration [VA] 
hospitals) by Ernest Bors and Estin Comarr. 75 These units inte -
grated medical care and physical rehabilitation and exemplified a 
comprehensive approach. However, as these units cared only for 
acutely injured veterans, their impact was limited. For the next 
several decades, care for nonveterans remained fragmented in the 
United States; it developed into the comprehensive model that  
we see today only after the US government began to support the 
development of the Model Systems for SCI.
John Young, a US physiatrist who had visited Guttmann in the 
early 1960s and had been exposed to Guttmann’s philosophy of 
continuing SCI care throughout life, became the first recipient of 
a grant for a demonstration Model System in 1970. The program 
that Young initially developed and the Model Systems created 
subsequently have had a remarkable influence over the years on 
how SCI care is delivered in the United States.
Model Systems of Care
At the same time that special trauma systems were being developed 
in the United States, differences in the outcomes these systems 
achieved as well as the outcomes achieved in a few SCI rehabilita-
tion units were being noticed by leaders in health care. These 
trauma systems required that injured persons be handled at the 
scene of injury by well-trained emergency medical technicians us-
ing state-of-the-art equipment and transportation vehicles. The 
patients were subsequently triaged in specially designated trauma 
centers, which met strict criteria for emergency and acute trauma 
care, rather than being taken to the nearest hospital. The success of 
the trauma systems highlighted the fact that inadequate training, 
experience, and number of staff—as well as a lack of appropriate 
facilities and equipment—not only placed the life of a severely 
injured person at risk but also often resulted in the development of 
complications and poor functional outcomes. It was therefore  
hypothesized that if optimal care was provided from the very onset 
of a major disabling injury and for as long as primary and second-
ary medical problems and disability lasted, functional outcomes 
would be improved and the cost of care reduced.
From its beginning as a single demonstration project in 1970, the 
Model Spinal Cord Injury System subsequently expanded; in 2024, 
there were 18 such systems and 3 follow-up centers sponsored by the 
National Institute on Disability, Independent Living, and Rehabili-
tation Research, under the US Department of Health and Human 
Services. Each funded system had to meet four basic requirements: 
(1) integrated clinical components, including emergency medical 
services, a trauma center, comprehensive rehabilitation services for 
both inpatients and outpatients, including vocational and job place-
ment services, and lifelong follow-up and health maintenance  
programs; (2) patient data collection and submission to a National 
SCI Model System Database; (3) research consistent with the funder 
priorities; (4) dissemination of the research.
As a result, the SCI Model Systems have been instrumental in 
developing standards of care and new treatments; conducting 
epidemiological, health services, and outcomes research; and 
producing thousands of publications and training materials. The 
National SCI Model System Database has been in existence since 
1973 and includes data regarding approximately 51,000 persons 
with SCI. 222 Based on this model, SCI databases have been  
developed in many other countries around the world as well as 
one spanning several European countries called the European 
Multicenter Study about SCI (EMSCI) database.
Subspecialty of Spinal Cord Injury Medicine
Most physicians providing nonsurgical care for people with SCI in 
the United States have been physiatrists. In the past, most such 
physicians developed their special knowledge by providing care 
rather than by specific training. The creation of a subspecialty of 
SCI medicine was first advocated in the late 1970s and gained 
momentum in the early 1990s. Through the concerted efforts of 
many individuals and organizations, the American Board of Medi-
cal Specialties gave its approval in 1995 for the establishment of 
such a subspecialty. After successfully completing an Accreditation 
Council for Graduate Medical Education (ACGME)–accredited 
SCI Medicine fellowship and passing a written exam administered 
by the American Board of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation 
(ABPMR), physicians receive SCI Medicine subspecialty certifica-
tion through the ABPMR. Several hundred have been certified in 
the subspecialty since the first examination was held in 1999.
Epidemiology
Incidence and Prevalence
Of all developed regions in the world that track SCI, North Amer-
ica has the highest recorded annual incidence of traumatic SCI. The 
incidence in the United States is approximately 54 cases per million 
population (or approximately 18,000 per year), followed closely by 
Canada, which has an incidence of 53 cases per million. 188,222 In 
other developed regions of the world, the incidence is considerably 
lower, varying from 19 and 24 cases per million in France and 
Spain, respectively, to between 12 and 14 cases per million within 
the Netherlands, Qatar, Ireland, Finland, and Australia.29
The exact number of persons with SCI currently alive in the 
United States (prevalence) is a matter of dispute; however, given 
the similar incidences of SCI in Canada and the United States, an 
estimate of the prevalence based on data from Canada,29 where the 
prevalence is approximately 910 cases per million, leads one to 
conclude that there are approximately 305,000 persons, with a 
range from 257,000 to 388,000 persons, in the United States liv-
ing with SCI.221 This figure is based on an estimated US popula -
tion of 335 million.243
Similar to the incidence data, the prevalence of traumatic SCI 
in other regions of the world is considerably lower, ranging from 
a prevalence of 280 cases per million in Finland to 681 cases per 
million in Australia.29
Children below 16 years of age account for 3% to 5% of the 
total number of SCIs that occur each year, whereas individuals 
below 20 years of age account for 15% to 20% of these SCIs. 49
Age at Time of Injury, Sex, and Marital Status
The incidence of traumatic SCI is bimodal, being highest among 
young adults and older individuals (age . 65 years). 188 In the 
United States, the incidence of traumatic SCI in older adults (age 
. 65 years) approaches 90 cases per million. Moreover, there is an 
increasing incidence of traumatic SCI in older adults that is not 
seen in any other age group throughout the world. 188 The mean 
age at injury has increased from 29 years in the 1970s in  
the United States to 44 years in the 2020s, slightly exceeding the

increase in median age of the US general population during this 
time.70,221 The majority of traumatic SCIs occur in males, 70% to 
80% overall, depending on the database queried. 188,222 The ratio 
of male to female SCI is equal to the age of 5 years, after which 
the ratio begins to favor males, exceeding 80% for those between 
16 and 20 years. However, with age, especially in those over  
65 years, the ratio narrows again, with females accounting for 
44% of SCI cases after the age of 65 years.
For those married at the time of SCI, the divorce rate is in-
creased after SCI as compared with that in the general population, 
especially during the first 3 postinjury years. The annual marriage 
rate is also lower for single individuals with SCI than for indi -
viduals without SCI.
70
Causes of Spinal Cord Injury
The most common causes of SCI worldwide in descending order of 
incidence are transport accidents, falls, violence, and sports. This 
varies among regions of the world, with transport accidents respon-
sible for approximately 50% of cases in Europe and 40% of cases in 
North America, Southeast Asia, and the Mediterranean. Falls are 
responsible for approximately 30% of the cases in North America 
and Europe but for over 40% in Southeast Asia and the Mediter-
ranean. The National Spinal Cord Injury Statistical Center 
(NSCISC) has created an online informational calculator that sum-
marizes SCI Model System data, allowing users to search for leading 
causes of SCI according to date range, race/ethnicity, and sex.
157
Although transport accidents are a significant cause of SCI in all 
age groups, falls are the most common cause of SCI in those above 
the age of 60 years. High falls (from . 1 m) are more common in 
younger persons, whereas low falls (from , 1 m) are more common 
for those over 45 years.
29 SCI in older individuals is often related to 
cervical spinal stenosis and is caused by a relatively minor trauma, 
such as a fall at home or in the street or a low-velocity motor vehicle 
accident. Violence as a cause is also disparate in its presence, being 
responsible for approximately 14% of the cases in the United 
States,
221 but less than 2% in Canada and Australia. 29 Of persons 
who suffer traumatic SCI in the United States, a disproportionate 
amount are non-Hispanic Black, with 24% of traumatic SCI affect-
ing non-Hispanic Black persons, nearly double that of the Black 
population in the United States.
89 Nearly 30% of traumatic SCIs in 
the United States in young males were caused by violence, with 
nearly 43% of those cases being in Black males (Fig. 50.1).

Furthermore, persons in minority groups (Black, Hispanic, 
etc.) in the United States are less likely to receive decompressive 
spinal surgery, less likely to receive acute inpatient rehabilita -
tion, and more likely to have medical complications as a result 
of their SCI.
97,190
The etiologies for children parallel those of younger adults 
with the exception that over 30% of these injuries are related to 
sports, with diving being the most common sports-related cause.
26 
Sports lead to more SCIs in males than in females only after the 
age of 13 years. Among those under 1 year of age who develop 
traumatic SCI, medical and surgical causes are most common; 
whereas transport accidents account for approximately 65% of 
cases for all those under 5 years of age. Unique causes of SCI in 
children include lap belt injuries, birth trauma, child abuse inju -
ries, and injuries of the craniovertebral junction.
Children younger than 8 years have a significantly higher inci-
dence of spinal cord injury without radiological abnormalities 
(SCIWORA), delayed onset of neurological deficits (ranging 
from 30 minutes to 4 days), and more neurologically complete 
lesions than do older children and adults.
248 Among children 
below 10 years of age, SCIWORA is seen in 60%; but it occurs 
in only 20% of those who are older.
248
Neonatal SCI is reported to occur in 1 of 60,000 births and is 
usually associated with breech presentations, leading most com-
monly to a lower cervical or upper thoracic cord lesion.
193 How-
ever, upper cervical SCI is associated with a cephalic presentation 
and delivery.
Neurological Level and Extent of  
Neurological Deficit
According to both the National SCI database (18 Model Systems 
of care in the United States) and the Nationwide Emergency De-
partment Sample (NEDS) database (980 hospital-based emer -
gency departments in 29 US states), tetraplegia is more common 
than paraplegia, accounting for 52% to 59% of SCI cases.
216,222 
The percentages of cases that are complete injuries, 31% and 11% 
of the SCI cases in the National SCI and NEDS databases, respec-
tively, are notably different.
216,222 This is not surprising given the 
composition of the contributing centers to each database. The 
most recent data from the National SCI database shows incom-
plete tetraplegia as the most common cause (47.4%) followed by 
incomplete paraplegia (20%), complete paraplegia (19.7%), and 
complete tetraplegia (12.3%).
221 Older individuals are more likely 
to have incomplete neurological lesions, which is especially true 
for lesions in the cervical region.
Life Expectancy, Morbidity, and Causes of Death
Life expectancy for persons with SCI remains below that of per -
sons without SCI. People with SCI are two to five times more 
likely to die prematurely.
29 The mortality rate is highest during 
the first postinjury year and declines significantly thereafter. Sig -
nificant predictors of mortality include level and completeness of 
injury, age at time of injury, and the need for a ventilator to 
breathe. Additional factors that affect longevity after the first 
postinjury year include low life satisfaction, poor health, emo -
tional distress, functional dependency, and poor adjustment to 
disability.
152 The NSCISC website provides annual updates on life 
expectancy after the onset of SCI, based on neurological level and 
ventilatory dependency ( Table 50.1). The NSCISC has also  
developed and maintains an online calculator that can be used to

estimate the life expectancy of a person with SCI who has already 
survived at least 1 year. 163 It should be noted that these life  
expectancy estimates do not differentiate among the many impor-
tant variables that can also significantly affect survival, such as 
medical comorbidities, access to medical and nursing care, and 
social support, all of which might affect a specific individual’s life 
expectancy.
Secondary conditions of SCI are no longer the main cause of 
death of people with SCI in high-income countries. Diseases of 
the respiratory system, especially pneumonia, are the leading 
cause of death both during the first postinjury year and in subse -
quent years (Table 50.2). The fifth most common cause of death, 
“other heart disease,” is thought to reflect deaths that are appar -
ently caused by heart attacks in younger persons without apparent 
underlying heart or vascular disease or cardiac dysrhythmia. 70
After SCI, older individuals (age . 65 years) have higher rates 
of complications, including pneumonia, respiratory insufficiency, 
pulmonary embolism (PE), renal stones, and gastrointestinal 
bleeding.215
Anatomy, Mechanics, and Syndromes of 
Traumatic Injury
Because the bony vertebral column elongates more than the spinal 
cord during embryological development, the spinal cord most 
commonly terminates at the level of the L1–L2 intervertebral 
disc. However, it is important to note that because of natural

as caudal as the L3 vertebral body. The individual spinal cord seg-
ments do not line up with the corresponding bony levels of the 
same number ( Fig. 50.2). This is especially evident in the lower 
thoracic and lumbar spine, where the L1–L5 spinal segments are 
adjacent to the T11–T12 vertebrae, and the S1–S5 spinal

segments are adjacent to the L1 vertebra. This concept can also be 
used when radiological studies are being evaluated to correlate the 
neurological level of injury (NLI) to the appropriate level of bony 
damage (e.g., a T11 burst fracture with cord compression would 
be expected to cause an NLI at L1 or L2 rather than at T11).
The tapered end of the spinal cord, which contains the sacral 
cord segments, is called the conus medullaris. The collection of 
long lumbar and sacral roots found in the canal distal to the co -
nus medullaris is called the cauda equina because it resembles a 
horse’s tail. The meninges of the spinal cord include the pia ma-
ter, which is a vascular membrane covering the spinal cord, the 
arachnoid membrane, and the dura mater. The subarachnoid 
space, also called the intrathecal space, contains cerebrospinal 
fluid (CSF). While the pia mater remains flush against the spinal 
cord, CSF fills the overlying space and pushes the arachnoid di-
rectly against the dura mater, forming what is tantamount to a 
single layer. The caudal margin of the layer formed by the dura 
mater and arachnoid creates the inferior extent of the intrathecal

1103Chapter 50  Spinal Cord Injury
into two horns on each side called the anterior (ventral) and pos-
terior (dorsal) horns. The posterior horn contains cell bodies of 
sensory neurons, whereas the anterior horn contains cell bodies of 
interneurons and motor neurons.
The white matter is subdivided into three columns on each 
side called the anterior, lateral, and posterior columns. The col -
umns are further subdivided into tracts. The gracilis tract, located 
in the medial posterior column, contains fibers from the T7–S5 
dermatomes that relay touch, vibration, and position sense. The 
cuneatus tract, located in the lateral posterior column rostral to 
T6, contains fibers from dermatomes above T7 that relay touch, 
vibration, and position sense. These tracts, comprising the poste -
rior columns, ascend ipsilaterally to the medulla. The lateral  
spinothalamic tract, located peripherally in the lateral column, 
contains fibers that relay pain and temperature sensations; this 
tract ascends contralaterally to the thalamus. The lateral cortico-
spinal tract is located centrally and posteriorly in the lateral col -
umn. This tract contains fibers, most of which emanate from the 
motor cortex, that are responsible for voluntary and reflex move -
ment. Approximately 90% of the corticospinal tract fibers cross 
midline in the caudal medulla, forming the pyramidal decussa -
tions, and descend contralaterally in the lateral corticospinal tract 
to terminate on interneurons and alpha and gamma motor neu -
rons in the spinal cord. The remaining corticospinal fibers, located 
in the medial anterior column, do not cross midline in the me -
dulla but descend ipsilaterally in the anterior corticospinal tract. 
These fibers ultimately cross midline segmentally near their termi-
nations on interneurons and alpha and gamma motor neurons in 
the spinal cord. Damage to different parts of the spinal cord  
results in damage to different tracts and as a result present with 
varying degrees of weakness or sensory loss. For example, Brown-
Séquard syndrome is an injury of the spinal cord in which one 
side is damaged more than the other (Fig. 50.5), resulting in rela-
tively greater ipsilateral weakness and position sense loss but with 
contralateral pain and loss of temperature sensation.
A corticospinal neuron is known as an upper motor neuron 
(UMN). The motor neuron to which it synapses in the spinal 
cord, which exits the spinal cord to innervate muscle, is known as 
a lower motor neuron (LMN). If damage to the UMNs and 
LMNs within the spinal cord is localized to a few segmental levels 
anywhere rostral to the conus medullaris (see later), a constella -
tion of signs and symptoms develops that is often called the 
UMN syndrome. This includes loss of voluntary movement, 
spasticity, hyperreflexia, clonus, and development of a Babinski 
sign. If, in addition, there is damage to a significant number of 
LMNs below the level of injury, loss of voluntary movement  
occurs without the subsequent development of the other compo-
nents of the UMN syndrome. Examples of this type, defined as 
LMN injuries, include an SCI caused by an extensive vascular 
insult to the spinal cord, an injury occurring at the conus medul -
laris, or an injury occurring at the cauda equina. The conus 
medullaris syndrome involves an injury of the sacral spinal cord 
and the lumbar nerve roots within the spinal canal ( Fig. 50.6), 
resulting in areflexic bladder, bowel, and lower limbs. Conus 
medullaris lesions localized to the proximal sacral cord can occa -
sionally show a preserved sacral reflex, such as the bulbocaverno -
sus (BC) reflex. The cauda equina syndrome involves an injury to 
the lumbosacral roots within the spinal canal (Fig. 50.7), resulting 
in areflexic bladder, bowel, and lower limbs.
After an acute UMN-predominant SCI, initial development of 
the UMN syndrome is delayed by a process called spinal shock, 
whereby there is a transient suppression and gradual return of

Blood is supplied to the spinal cord through two posterior 
spinal arteries, a single anterior spinal artery, and several segmen -
tal radicular arteries (Fig. 50.8). The posterior spinal arteries 
branch from the vertebral arteries and travel along the posterior 
surface of the spinal cord to supply the posterior third of the spi -
nal cord. T wo anterior spinal arteries also branch from the verte-
bral arteries, but these quickly unite to form a single artery that 
travels along the anterior surface of the spinal cord to supply the 
anterior two-thirds of the spinal cord. The anterior spinal artery 
and the posterior spinal arteries are dependent on contributions 
from the segmental radicular arteries along the spinal cord to 
maintain an adequate blood supply to the spinal cord. The seg-
mental radicular arteries travel through the intervertebral foram -
ina from the aorta and divide into anterior and posterior branches 
that eventually anastomose with their respective spinal arteries. 
These radicular arteries are not all identical in size or distribution. 
In the upper thoracic region between T1 and T4, there is little 
overlap between radicular arterial supplies. Between T12 and L2, 
there is an anterior radicular artery, more dominant than its 
neighbors, called the artery of Adamkiewicz. This artery, usually 
found on the left side of the body, provides an important blood 
supply to the caudal two-thirds of the spinal cord. On reaching 
the anterior surface of the spinal cord, the artery of Adamkiewicz 
divides into a small ascending and larger descending branch, the 
latter of which travels down to the level of the conus medullaris,

where it forms an anastomotic circle with the terminal branches 
of the posterior spinal arteries. The region between T1 and T4 and 
that between T12 and L2 are areas particularly prone to ischemic 
damage because of the importance of individual radicular arteries. 
The ischemic damage often affects the anterior portion of the 
spinal cord more than the posterior portion because of the nature 
of the single anterior and dual posterior blood supplies. In this 
situation, the corticospinal and spinothalamic tracts are affected, 
whereas the gracilis tract is often spared. This leads to a syndrome 
of paraplegia—with loss of pain and temperature sensation but 
relative sparing of touch and position sensation—called the ante -
rior cord syndrome (Fig. 50.9).
Pathophysiology of Acute Spinal Cord Injury
The mechanical damage caused at the time of injury, called the 
primary injury to the spinal cord, starts a sequence of pathological 
events collectively referred to as secondary injury. These secondary 
changes begin within seconds of the primary injury and continue 
for several weeks thereafter.
262
Mechanical disruption of the spinal cord’s vasculature leads to 
the development of microhemorrhages in the gray and white mat-
ter, interstitial edema, and the release of coagulation factors and

vasospasm of the microvasculature of the spinal cord, causing tis -
sue hypoxia and impaired neuronal homeostasis. At the cellular 
level, there are changes in ion concentrations, peroxidation of 
membrane lipids, formation of free radicals, and  release of toxic 
excitatory neurotransmitters.
262
In response to injury, neutrophils initially migrate to the site of 
injury, where they can contribute to cellular injury by producing 
lysosomal enzymes and oxygen radicals. These are followed by 
macrophages that phagocytose cell debris.
205 Demyelination of 
white matter tracts begins within 24 hours of injury, but calcium-
mediated axonal dieback of axons destined for wallerian degen -
eration (WD) can be seen as early as 4 hours after primary  
injury.
192 Full WD occurs by 3 weeks, 253 but advanced magnetic 
resonance imaging (MRI) techniques such as diffusion-weighted 
imaging and diffusion tensor imaging show early phases of WD 
within a few days.
50
Spinal Mechanics and Stability
White and Panjabi260 defined clinical instability as “the loss of the 
ability of the spine under physiological loads to maintain relation-
ships between vertebrae in such a way that there is not initial 
damage or subsequent irritation to the spinal cord or nerve roots 
and, in addition, there is no development of incapacitating defor-
mity or pain caused by structural changes.” One easily conceptu-
alized model for thoracolumbar stability, which has often been 
applied in the middle and lower cervical spine as well, was devel -
oped by Denis.
67 The model divides the spine into three columns: 
anterior, middle, and posterior (Fig. 50.10). The anterior column 
is composed of the anterior longitudinal ligament, the anterior 
portion of the vertebral body, and the anterior portion of the  
• Fig. 50.9 Anterior cord syndrome. A large disc herniation compresses 
the anterior aspect of the spinal cord, leaving the dorsal columns intact. 
(Redrawn from Tator C. Classification of spinal cord injury based on neu-
rological presentation. In: Narayan R, Wilberger J, Povlishock J, eds. 
Neurotrauma. McGraw-Hill; 1996.)
MiddlePosterior Anterior
• Fig. 50.10 The three-column concept of spinal anatomy. (Redrawn from 
Ferguson RL, Allen Jr BL. A mechanistic classification of thoracolumbar 
spine fractures. Clin Orthop. 1984;189:77-88.)
annulus fibrosus or disc. The middle column is composed of the 
posterior portion of the vertebral body, the posterior portion of 
the annulus fibrosus, and the posterior longitudinal ligament. The 
posterior column is composed of the pedicles, facet joints, lami -
nae, supraspinous ligament, interspinous ligament, facet joint 
capsule, and ligamentum flavum. When the integrity of the 
middle and either the anterior or posterior column is affected, the 
spine is likely to be unstable.
67 The columns can be compromised 
by either fracture or ligamentous disruption. Gunshot wounds, 
because of the nature of the injury, can affect more than one col -
umn, but the spine can still remain stable. It should also be noted 
that SCI can occur without obvious radiographic findings.
Over the years, numerous spinal fracture classification schemes 
have been proposed to facilitate and standardize the assessment of 
stability. One of the most accepted and validated systems is the 
AO Spine Injury Classification System, which consists of four 
regional classifications developed by the AO Spine Classification 
group.
213 These systems are based on different identifiable mor -
phological types of injury, including compression injuries (type 
A), tension band injuries (type B), translational injuries (type C), 
and injuries within the cervical subaxial spine as well as the types 
of bilateral (type BL) and facet (type F) injuries.
213,244,245 In these 
classifications, subtypes for patient-specific modifiers and neuro -
logical status also exist to make the classification more com -
prehensive and to aid in surgical decision making. 213,244,245 By 
identifying the specific morphology of the fractures, neurological 
status, and any clinical/special modifiers, fractures can be strati -
fied by severity.
213 The final classifications can be condensed into 
short codes with letters (e.g., A, B, and C types) and numbers 
(e.g., indicating the severity of A, B, and C). eFigs. 50.1 and 50.2 
further explicate these classification systems.
Fractures or dislocations in the thoracic and lumbar spine most 
commonly involve the T12 and L1 vertebrae, respectively. Com -
mon mechanisms include compression-flexion, distraction- 
flexion, translation, and torsion-flexion. Axial loading of a flexed 
spine can cause several different patterns of injury depending on 
the vector of force. There might be only compression of the ante-
rior column, leading to a compression fracture, or, with a greater 
compressive force, compression of the anterior column with dis -
traction of the posterior elements. If the vector of force causes the 
axis of rotation to be anterior to the vertebral body, a Chance-type 
distraction can occur with distraction of all three columns 
through the bony vertebra alone ( Fig. 50.11), through the

ligamentous structures alone, or through a combination of bony 
and ligamentous structures. In addition, there can be compression 
of all three columns with retropulsion of the middle column into 
the spinal canal, which often causes SCI. T ranslation of adjacent 
vertebrae—as occurs, for example, when a person falls from a 
height and strikes part of the torso on an immovable object—is 
the injury pattern most likely to cause SCI. If there is translation 
greater than 25% of the width of a vertebra, ligamentous struc -
tures in all three columns are probably disrupted. Compression 
and rotation of the anterior column and distraction and rotation 
of the posterior column cause a torsion-flexion injury, where the 
facets and the anterior longitudinal ligament are usually dis-
rupted, likely resulting in an SCI.
Lap belt injuries in motor vehicle accidents occur most often 
in children weighing less than 60 pounds who are wearing a regu-
lar lap belt above the pelvic rim.
249 A clinical triad of abdominal 
wall bruising, intraabdominal injuries, and SCI at or close to the 
thoracolumbar junction is often seen in these children.
248
Fractures or dislocations in the cervical spine are usually caused 
by excessive forceful flexion, extension, or axial loading. An 
abrupt deceleration, commonly seen in motor vehicle accidents, 
causes a person’s head to be propelled forward on a relatively im-
mobilized torso, leading to a flexion-type injury. Conversely, 
abrupt acceleration causes an extension-type injury. Axial loading 
is also a common mechanism contributing to flexion or extension 
injuries in the cervical spine, as occurs when a diver’s head strikes 
the bottom of a pool. Knowledge of the mechanism of injury is 
important for recognizing injuries that might not be seen easily 
on imaging. For example, flexion injuries can cause disruption of 
the ligaments in the posterior column that might not be apparent 
on plain radiographs.
Craniovertebral junction injuries (i.e., atlantooccipital or at-
lantoaxial) are relatively common in children but not adults. They 
often result in immediate death, and the SCI can go undetected.
A Jefferson fracture, originally described by Sir Geoffrey Jef-
ferson, is a burst fracture of the atlas (C1 vertebra). This is caused 
by axial compression, which can occur, for example, when a foot-
ball player spears another player with his helmet (Fig. 50.12).
A hangman’s fracture is a traumatic spondylolisthesis of the 
axis (C2 vertebra). It is caused by BL-type fractures through the 
pars interarticularis of the axis that result from hyperextension 
and axial compression, as can occur in an abrupt deceleration 
when a person’s forehead strikes the windshield. A fracture of the 
• Fig. 50.11 A chance fracture (arrow). (Redrawn from Schultz RJ. The 
Language of Fractures. Williams & Wilkins; 1990.)
odontoid process of the axis can be caused by hyperflexion, hyper-
extension, or excessive lateral bending. The traditional classifica -
tion of odontoid fractures includes three types. Type 1 is a fracture 
through the tip of the odontoid, type 2  is a fracture through the 
base of the odontoid, and type 3 is a fracture that extends from 
the base of the odontoid into the axis proper.
Hyperflexion of the subaxial cervical spine (C3–C7) can cause 
an anterior subluxation, a simple compression fracture, BL facet 
dislocations, a flexion teardrop fracture, or a clay shoveler’s frac -
ture. A flexion teardrop fracture is characterized by retropulsion of 
the larger portion of a vertebral body into the spinal canal, de -
tached from an anterior fragment (teardrop); it is associated with 
posterior facet and ligamentous disruption (Fig. 50.13). Flexion 
teardrop fractures are often associated with an anterior cord syn-
drome if not a complete SCI. A clay shoveler’s fracture is an avul-
sion fracture of the spinous process of C6, C7, or T1 and not 
typically associated with neurological injury. Hyperflexion with 
rotation often causes a unilateral facet dislocation.
Hyperextension of the subaxial cervical spine typically dis-
tracts the anterior column of the spine and compresses the pos -
terior column. Anterior distraction often disrupts the anterior

longitudinal ligament, whereas posterior compression causes the 
ligamentum flavum to buckle into the spinal canal. If the spinal 
cord is pinched between the vertebral body and the ligamentum 
flavum and/or hypertrophied facet joints, a central cord syn -
drome often develops. This syndrome, occurring only with cervi-
cal spinal cord lesions, is characterized by sacral sensory sparing 
and greater weakness in the upper limbs as compared with the 
lower limbs. A hyperextension teardrop fracture is characterized 
by an avulsion of the anteroinferior aspect of the vertebral body 
above the hyperextension injury by the anterior longitudinal 
ligament without retropulsion of a vertebral body into the spinal 
canal. Compression of the posterior column during excessive 
forceful hyperextension can also result in fractures of the lamina.
Finally, and not uncommonly, significant axial loading of the 
subaxial cervical spine causes a burst fracture whereby an interver-
tebral disc implodes through the superior end plate of the verte -
bral body below, causing this vertebral body to burst into multiple 
fragments. These fractures usually include at least two columns, 
are generally unstable, and are often associated with SCI.
Classification of Spinal Cord Injury
The basic diagnosis of SCI can be made by performing a neuro -
logical examination of the motor and sensory components of the 
somatic nervous system. The International Standards for Neuro -
logical Classification of Spinal Cord Injury (ISNCSCI) provides a 
procedure for classifying SCI in this manner.148 A more complete 
classification can be obtained by assessing the autonomic nervous 
system function in addition to the somatic nervous system. The 
International Standards to Document Autonomic Function After 
Spinal Cord Injury (ISAFSCI) provides a procedure for classifying 
SCI in this manner.255 Comprehensive online e-learning modules 
(the International Standards T raining and the Autonomic Stan -
dards T raining e-programs) are available through the American 
Spinal Injury Association (ASIA) website.131
The International Standards for Neurological 
Classification of Spinal Cord Injury
The examination is safe to perform soon after SCI, even in per -
sons with an unstable spine. The initial and subsequent ISNCSCI 
exam is performed with the injured individual in the supine posi-
tion. The procedure includes a systematic evaluation of all the 
dermatomes and extremity myotomes. Because SCI usually affects 
the spinal cord at a discrete site, determining the last intact sen -
sory and motor level can reliably and accurately determine the 
NLI. A complete injury is defined within the ISNCSCI as an in -
jury in which there is no sacral sparing and no sensory or motor 
function in the lowest sacral segment; this includes deep anal 
pressure (DAP), sensation (light touch [LT] and pinprick [PP]) at 
the anal mucocutaneous junction, or a voluntary anal contraction 
(VAC) of the external anal sphincter. An incomplete injury is 
defined as an injury in which there is sacral sparing (i.e., at least 
partial sensory or motor function in the lowest sacral segment).
The sensory portion of the neurological examination tests LT 
and PP in each of the 28 dermatomes on each side of the body as 
well as DAP as part of the rectal exam. LT is elicited by a wisp of 
cotton or a fingertip, and PP is elicited with a disposable safety 
pin. Using the cheek of the face as the normal control point, LT 
and PP are graded on a 3-point scale (0–2), with 0 for absent, 1 
for impaired (hypoesthesia or hyperesthesia), and 2 for normal 
sensation. A common pitfall is scoring a 1 for impaired PP sensa-
tion when it should actually be scored a 0. This scenario occurs 
when an examiner asks the subject if the pin is felt, the subject 
responds yes, and the examiner fails to clarify that the subject is 
actually feeling PP rather than pressure when being poked with 
the pin. A score of 1 on PP testing requires the subject to differ -
entiate sharp from dull. If there is doubt in the mind of the exam-
iner about the ability of an individual to differentiate sharp from 
dull, performing the test 10 times and obtaining at least 8 of 10 
correct responses can provide confidence in the assessment. DAP 
is recorded as either present (yes) or absent (no).
The motor portion of the neurological examination includes 
the testing of a key muscle function for strength on a 6-point scale 
(0–5) for each of 10 myotomes, 5 in the upper limb and 5 in the 
lower limb on each side of the body, as well as testing for contrac-
tion of the external anal sphincter. The muscles are examined in a 
rostral-to-caudal sequence in order of the myotomes. The five key 
upper limb muscles represent the C5–T1 myotomes, and the key 
lower limb muscles represent the L2–S1 myotomes. Common 
pitfalls in motor testing include not recognizing the following: 
active shoulder external rotation, which can allow an individual to 
utilize momentum and gravity to extend the elbow; passive finger 
flexion, which can occur with tenodesis; finger extension by the 
long finger extensors, which can cause slight abduction of the 
small finger; abdominal and/or hip adductor activation, which 
can cause slight hip flexion; and long toe extension, which can 
cause ankle dorsiflexion. The testing of every key muscle should 
begin in the grade 3 testing position. If the strength of the muscle 
is shown to have full range of motion (ROM) against gravity 
without resistance (grade 3), the muscle should be tested for grade 
4 (full ROM against resistance but not normal strength) and 
grade 5 (normal) strength. Conversely, if the muscle is shown to 
have less than antigravity strength when tested in the grade 3 test-
ing position, the muscle should be tested for grade 2 strength (full 
ROM with gravity eliminated). If the muscle is shown to have not 
even grade 2 strength, the muscle is graded 0 (no visible or pal -
pable contraction) or 1 (any visible or palpable contraction).  
Before strength is tested in a particular key muscle, the ROM for 
the joint that is crossed by that particular muscle should be tested. 
If 50% of the ROM is unavailable, the muscle cannot be accu -
rately scored and is recorded NT for not testable. When a key 
muscle is unavailable for testing, the muscle should be scored NT , 
and the reason noted. In the presence of spasticity, the examiner 
should attempt to relax the muscle to allow for accurate grading. 
If this is not possible, the muscle should be scored NT . The pres-
ence of contractures may also cause confusion and inaccuracy in 
muscle grading, and any muscle with contractures should be 
scored NT . T o document non–SCI-related motor and sensory 
impairments at a particular myotome or dermatome, respectively, 
an asterisk (*) is appended to any actual nonnormal motor or 
sensory grade (0–4 for motor and 0–1 for sensory) at a particular 
myotome or dermatome whenever a non-SCI condition impacts 
the motor and sensory grading at that particular myotome or 
dermatome. If any asterisk-appended motor or sensory grades  
affect calculated ISNCSCI variables (e.g., motor or sensory levels 
or scores or an ASIA Impairment Score [AIS]), these ISNCSCI 
variables are also appended with an asterisk.
Once the examiner has performed a complete sensory, motor, 
and anorectal examination, the sensory levels, motor levels, single 
NLI, completeness of injury, AIS, and (if pertinent) zone of par -
tial preservation (ZPP) are determined. The sensory level is the 
lowest dermatomal level with normal sensation to PP and LT ,

with all dermatomes above being normal. The motor level is the 
level of the lowest innervated muscle having grade 3 or better 
strength where all muscles above are graded 5. The reason for this 
is that even though each of the key muscles has one root listed 
(myotome), the key muscle is innervated by two roots (i.e., C5 
and C6 for elbow flexion), with each successive key muscle over -
lapping the muscle above in either the cervical or lumbar region. 
If a key muscle has a motor strength grade of 5, it is innervated 
by two intact nerve segments. If a key muscle has a motor strength 
grade of 3 or 4 (and the key muscle above has a motor strength 
grade of 5), it is innervated by at least one intact nerve segment, 
the segment for which that key muscle is named. If a key muscle 
has a motor strength grade of 2 or less, neither of its nerve seg -
ments is intact. For myotomes where there are no designated key 
muscles to test, motor function is assumed to be normal where 
sensory function is normal. The single NLI is defined as the most 
caudal segment of the spinal cord with normal sensation and mo-
tor function bilaterally.
The AIS stratifies SCI into five categories of severity, labeled A 
through E, based on the degree of motor and sensory loss. An SCI 
that results in the absence of any sensory or motor function in the 
sacral segments S4–S5 would have an AIS category of A and be 
designated as complete. For an SCI where sensation is preserved 
in the sacral segments S4–S5 (sacral sparing) but there is no VAC 
or motor function more than three segments below the motor 
level, the AIS is B. For an SCI where sensation is preserved in the 
sacral segments S4–S5, there is VAC or motor function more than 
three segments below the motor level, but more than half the key 
muscles below the NLI have a muscle grade less than 3, the AIS 
is C. For individuals with AIS B, the presence of nonkey muscles 
greater than three segments below the motor level indicates an 
AIS C. Nonkey muscles can be used only to differentiate AIS B 
from AIS C. For an SCI where sensation is preserved in the sacral 
segments S4–S5 but at least half the key muscles below the NLI 
have a muscle grade of 3 or greater, the AIS is D. When sensory 
and motor function are normal, the AIS is E. AIS categories B 
through E designate incomplete injuries. If an examiner has uti -
lized a sensory or motor asterisk indicator during the examination 
score, the asterisk-tagged score should be specified in the com -
ment box available in the ISNCSCI examination scoring sheet. 
Scores that are tagged with an asterisk and are above the applica -
ble sensory or motor level will not affect classification of scoring 
in most cases; however, scores tagged with an asterisk at or below 
the relevant level are typically handled as not normal. In other 
words, they are interpreted as a non–SCI-related impairment  
superimposed upon a deficit caused by the SCI. Any classification 
variable (motor level, sensory level, NLI, or AIS grade) affected by 
an asterisk-tagged score should be designated with an asterisk on 
the final examination grading. This denotes that the classification 
results are subject to the clinical interpretation of the reported 
scores.
The ZPP indicates the most caudal myotome or dermatome 
with any motor or sensory function present when there is ab -
sence of motor or sensory function, respectively, in the lowest 
innervated segments. A motor ZPP is indicated only when there 
is no evidence of VAC irrespective of the presence of PP or LT 
sensation at S4–S5 or DAP; otherwise, it is not applicable and 
designated as such. Whereas a sensory ZPP is indicated only if 
there is absence of PP and LT sensation at S4–S5 and absence of 
DAP; otherwise, it is also not applicable. 10 There may be four 
distinct recordings for ZPP (sensory, right and left; motor, right 
and left).
Certain aspects must be taken into consideration when a child 
is being examined. There is a learning module on the ASIA Learn-
ing Center website addressing these areas.256
Scoring calculators that are freely available online have been 
developed for the ISNCSCI.88,134
International Standards to Document Autonomic 
Function After Spinal Cord Injury
The ISAFSCI should be administered in conjunction with the full 
ISNCSCI and should be considered any time there is a change in 
clinical intervention that may impact autonomic control of any 
organ system function. 255 The ISAFSCI documents markers of 
autonomic function for the cardiovascular, thermoregulatory, su-
domotor, bronchopulmonary, genitourinary, and gastrointestinal 
systems. For the cardiovascular system, blood pressure and pulse 
are serially measured in the supine and sitting positions. For the 
thermoregulatory system, serial temperatures are recorded. For 
the sudomotor system, changes in sweating above and below the 
NLI are assessed through self-report. For the bronchopulmonary 
system, use of invasive ventilation is noted, and forced vital capac-
ity (FVC) is measured. For the genitourinary system, the follow -
ing are documented: self-report awareness of bladder fullness and 
the ability to prevent bladder leakage, psychogenic sexual arousal, 
reflex genital arousal, and the ability to orgasm. For the gastroin -
testinal system, self-report awareness of bowel fullness and the 
ability to prevent bowel leakage are documented.
Nontraumatic Spinal Cord Injury
Nontraumatic SCI can be caused by a variety of diseases, includ -
ing neoplastic, infectious, inflammatory, vascular, degenerative 
(spondylosis), congenital, and toxic-metabolic disorders. Persons 
affected by nontraumatic SCI are clinically quite different from 
those with traumatic injuries. Because their injuries are generally 
less severe and almost always incomplete, they are associated with 
a far better prognosis for neurological improvement than are those 
with complete injuries. Also, unlike persons with traumatic SCI, 
persons with nontraumatic SCI are significantly more likely to 
have paraplegia than tetraplegia.117,174
Neoplastic Causes of Spinal Cord Injury
T umors associated with SCI can arise from either the neural ele -
ments in the spinal canal, such as the spinal cord or spinal nerves, 
or the structures comprising the spinal column, most commonly 
the vertebral bodies. T umors arising from the spine (extradural 
tumors) are much more common than those arising from neural 
elements (intradural tumors).
Classification of Spinal Tumors.  The most common method 
of classifying tumors relates to the anatomical location of tumor 
involvement ( Fig. 50.14 ). Spinal tumors are extradural when 
they arise from structures outside the dura, most commonly the 
vertebral body. A less likely origin is from the structures of the 
posterior bony arch or the soft tissues outside the dura. Most 
tumors metastatic to the spine are extradural, making up more 
than one-half of all spinal tumors. 52,109,226 The most common 
primary sites of metastatic tumors to the spine are lung, breast, 
prostate, and kidney. 2,109,121 The mechanisms of metastasis in -
clude direct extension of tumor from adjacent tissues and hema -
togenous spread through the Batson vertebral venous plexus, a 
valveless venous system draining the thoracic, abdominal, and 
pelvic viscera. Primary spinal tumors that are present in the ex -
tradural region make up less than 1% of all spinal tumors. These

1109Chapter 50  Spinal Cord Injury
include multiple myeloma, osteogenic sarcoma, vertebral heman-
gioma, chondrosarcoma, and chordoma. 109,121,226
T umors arising within the intradural space include those that 
are intramedullary (i.e., tumors arising from the parenchyma of 
the spinal cord) and those that are intradural but extramedullary. 
Intramedullary tumors are usually primary tumors, most com -
monly ependymomas and astrocytomas, which together make up 
75% of all intramedullary tumors.
109 Ependymomas tend to be 
well encapsulated and regularly shaped, in contrast to astrocyto-
mas, which tend to be irregularly shaped with multiple extensions 
into the cord parenchyma. Most intradural extramedullary  
tumors are both benign and primary; they include meningiomas 
and nerve sheath tumors such as schwannomas and neurofibro-
mas.
109 Those metastatic tumors that are seen can arise either by 
hematogenous spread or as drop metastases (i.e., lesions that ex -
tend directly from the CSF in association with malignant brain 
tumors such as medulloblastomas).
160
Clinical Presentation of Spinal Tumors. Pain is the most com-
mon presenting symptom of a spinal tumor. Such pain is often 
worse in the supine position, in contrast to the pain associated 
with degenerative spondylosis, which is usually worse in the up -
right position. If the tumor involves only skeletal structures, the 
pain is usually axial. If the tumor involves nerve roots, it typically 
occurs in a radicular distribution. If the tumor involves the spinal 
cord, the pain can present as at-level or below-level SCI pain. 
Constitutional symptoms such as night sweats, fevers, unex -
plained weight loss, and anorexia can suggest tumor as well.
Acute spinal cord compression is associated with rapid neuro -
logical decline and constitutes a medical emergency because it can 
rapidly progress to paraplegia or tetraplegia. When signs and 
symptoms of acute spinal cord compression related to neoplastic 
involvement of the spine are present, most patients will have sub-
stantial radiographic abnormalities. The syndrome of acute spinal 
cord compression related to spinal tumors most often results from 
the invasion of spinal structures by extradural metastases.
Management of Spinal Cord Compression by Tumor. Acute 
spinal cord compression is managed with corticosteroids, radiation, 
and surgical intervention. Corticosteroids, typically dexamethasone,

are administered to reduce tumor-related inflammatory changes and 
prostaglandin production.156 Radiation therapy is often used in cases 
of spinal cord compression resulting from soft tissue encroachment. 
It can be used alone in the setting of spinal stability or in combina-
tion with surgery. Radiation therapy is less often used for the treat-
ment of intradural or intramedullary tumors unless such tumors are 
deemed unresectable or when surgical resection is incomplete.
156 
Radiosensitive tumors involving the spine include lymphomas, small 
cell lung cancer, and multiple myeloma, whereas less radiosensitive 
tumors include breast, prostate, non–small cell lung, and renal cell 
cancers.
121 Complications of radiation directed to the spine include 
radiation myelopathy and radiation plexopathy. In the setting of 
acute spinal cord compression, the immediate goal of surgical 
treatment is decompression of the cord to preserve or improve 
neurological function.
For most intramedullary and intradural extramedullary tu -
mors, surgical intervention is the most effective treatment. Epen-
dymomas, because of their encapsulated nature, can often be  
resected completely with good preservation of neurological func -
tion. In contrast, because astrocytomas are irregular and invasive 
without a clear plane for resection, the goal of surgery is a subtotal 
resection of clearly abnormal tissue. Intradural extramedullary 
meningiomas arise from the dura and are resected along with the 
involved dura after being accessed through a laminectomy. Nerve 
sheath tumors can be entirely intradural or, in the case of the 
neurofibroma, can have extradural extension through an enlarged 
neural foramen. In neurofibromatosis type 2, extensive intradural 
involvement throughout much of the spinal cord can be present. 
In such cases, tumor debulking rather than complete resection is 
usually the surgical goal.
Infectious and Inflammatory Causes of Spinal Cord Injury
Bacterial Infection. Bacteria can invade the vertebral body and 
epidural space either hematogenously or by direct extension from 
a contiguous focus of infection, causing osteomyelitis and spinal 
epidural abscess, respectively. Predisposing factors include underly-
ing diseases (e.g., diabetes mellitus, alcoholism, and infection with 
human immunodeficiency virus [HIV]), spinal abnormality or

intervention (e.g., degenerative joint disease, trauma, surgery, drug 
injection, placement of stimulator or catheter), or a local or sys -
temic source of infection (e.g., skin and soft tissue infection,  
urinary tract infection (UTI), sepsis, indwelling vascular access, 
intravenous drug use, epidural analgesia). The bacterium most 
commonly implicated in osteomyelitis and spinal epidural ab -
scesses is Staphylococcus aureus, which accounts for more than one-
half of all such infections.32,61,203 Although infection can be seen in 
any portion of the spine, the lumbar spine is the most common 
area for osteomyelitis and the thoracolumbar region for spinal 
epidural abscess because of the larger and fattier epidural space. 
Spine pain is by far the most common symptom of vertebral osteo-
myelitis and spinal epidural abscess, seen respectively in over 
90%106 and three-quarters203 of persons affected. Other symptoms 
can include fever or a neurological deficit related to spinal cord 
compression from vertebral body collapse or the presence of an 
epidural abscess. Laboratory markers of inflammation, such as the 
erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein, are very 
frequently found to be elevated with active infection. Isolation of 
the etiological pathogen is vital to treatment success. This can be 
accomplished by recovery of the organism in blood cultures or 
through cultures of tissue obtained from the spine or epidural  
abscess, either by needle or open biopsy. T reatment of vertebral 
osteomyelitis and spinal epidural abscess involves intravenous an-
tibiotic administration for at least 6 weeks. Surgical treatment for 
osteomyelitis is indicated when appropriate antibiotics have been 
ineffective, when there is spinal cord or nerve root compression 
causing a neurological deficit, or when there is spinal instability or 
spinal deformity. However, emergent surgical management with 
laminectomy and removal of abscess should be performed for spi-
nal epidural abscess unless the patient refuses therapy, has an unac-
ceptably high operative risk, or has a complete SCI of more than 
48-hour duration with low clinical or radiographic concern for an 
ascending lesion.242 A delay in surgery can lead to less neurological 
improvement after decompression as compared with those in 
whom surgical intervention occurs earlier.203
T uberculosis of the spine, also known as Pott disease, results 
from hematogenous spread of the bacterium Mycobacterium tu-
berculosis to the spine, typically from a pulmonary focus. Spinal 
tuberculosis is treated with at least two, and as many as four, an -
tituberculosis agents for a 6- to 12-month duration.
HIV and Human T-Lymphotropic Virus Infection.  Persons 
with HIV infection are susceptible to spinal cord disease, which 
can occur as vacuolar myelopathy (VM), primary HIV myelitis, 
or a result of opportunistic infections of the spinal cord. VM is 
the most frequent cause of nontraumatic SCI in HIV-seroposi -
tive individuals from highly resourced countries. It more likely 
occurs during later stages of HIV infection. Pathological findings 
of VM on autopsy include mainly lateral and posterior involve -
ment as well as middle to lower thoracic levels; there is symmetry 
early on with asymmetry developing later in more severe cases. 
VM is a diagnosis of exclusion and presents with an incomplete 
spastic paraplegia with loss of proprioception and vibration 
sense.
Human T-lymphotropic virus type 1 (HTLV-1) is another 
retrovirus that causes a progressive chronic myelopathy. The clini-
cal condition is a slowly progressive spastic paraplegia, which is 
referred to as both tropical spastic paraparesis and HTLV-1–  
associated myelopathy. The virus is transmitted through blood, 
sexual contacts, and from mother to child in breast milk. It occurs 
in the Caribbean, southern Japan, Central and South Africa, and 
regions of South America.106
Transverse Myelitis.  T ransverse myelitis (TM), 234 a clinical 
syndrome caused by inflammation of both sides of the spinal 
cord, is characterized by signs and symptoms of neurological 
dysfunction, including altered sensation, weakness, and often 
bowel and bladder dysfunction. Indicators of better prognosis 
include being younger when affected, experiencing an early 
onset of recovery, having a slower progression of sensory or mo -
tor symptoms over days and/or weeks, and retaining dorsal 
column function and deep tendon reflexes. Indicators of a 
poorer prognosis include an acute and rapid onset of deficits, 
severe neurological dysfunction, tetraplegia, bowel and bladder 
dysfunction, and a lack of recovery after 3 months. 209 When a 
person has a rapidly evolving myelopathy with no history of 
trauma or physical or radiographic evidence of a structural  
lesion, the differential diagnosis should include potential causes 
of myelitis, such as systemic lupus erythematosus, Sjögren syn -
drome, multiple sclerosis (MS), neuromyelitis optica (NMO), 
neurosarcoidosis, paraneoplastic syndromes, nutritional defi -
ciencies, vascular insufficiency, decompression sickness, and 
infection ( Table 50.3).
TM can progress over the course of several hours or a few 
weeks. Even when infection is thought to be a cause, causative 
organisms are rarely if ever isolated. Although TM can occur in 
any region of the spinal cord, the thoracic region is most com -
monly affected. In persons with TM, MRI scanning often shows 
spinal cord swelling, with a region of increased signal on  
T2-weighted images that correlates with the NLI. When the le -
sions are seen to extend longitudinally across many levels, TM can 
be characterized further as longitudinally extensive transverse 
myelitis (LETM). LETM is characteristic of NMO, among other 
diseases.268
Acute treatment of TM is aimed at halting the inflammatory 
process. The standard treatment is the administration of high-
dose intravenous methylprednisolone at 1000 mg/day for 3 to  
7 days. If the response is suboptimal, plasma exchange is recom -
mended to remove any humoral factors that may be contributing 
to the pathological process. Last, if there is still no improvement 
with plasma exchange, intravenous immunoglobulin, cyclophos -
phamide, rituximab, or azathioprine (the last two for NMO) may 
be of benefit.259
Neuromyelitis Optica.  NMO is an immune-mediated in -
flammatory disease of the central nervous system. It was first  
described in 1894 by Eugene Devic. Historically, there has been 
a long debate as to whether the syndrome typical for what is now 
called NMO was a distinct disease or was a clinical variant of 
MS. The identification of a biomarker for NMO, aquaporin-4 
antibody (AQP4-Ab), also termed NMO-IgG, helped to confirm 
NMO as a distinct disease. Nevertheless, because there is consen-
sus that NMO can also be seronegative for AQP4-Ab, the term 
NMO spectrum disorder  (NMOSD) is now commonly used. 
Consensus diagnostic criteria for NMOSD were published in 
2015 by the International Panel for NMO Diagnosis and a fol -
low-up was published by the Neuromyelitis Optica Study Group 
in 2023.136,263
NMO is characterized by a sudden onset of a varying degree 
of eye pain and vision loss (optic neuritis) as well as inflammation 
of the spinal cord resulting in limb weakness, numbness, and 
paresthesia as well as loss of bowel and bladder function and even 
sometimes prolonged bouts of hiccups. NMO and MS have many 
overlapping symptoms, and the differences are subtle. Symptoms 
are generally more severe after an NMO attack than an MS  
attack. NMO usually affects only the optic nerve and spinal cord,

whereas MS typically affects the brain, spinal cord, and optic 
nerve. In MS, individual episodes are usually mild; their cumula -
tive effects over time may or may not cause progressive disability. 
In NMO, the opposite is true; acute episodes are usually severe 
and, if untreated, can have devastating, irreversible effects on 
function.
NMO has a female predominance (9:1, compared with just 
2:1 in MS) and a peak presentation in the fourth decade,  
approximately 10 years later than MS. In contrast to MS, a 
higher proportion of the patients with NMO are non-White. 
The prognosis for NMO is worse than that for MS, with fre -
quent relapses. AQP4-Ab is clinically helpful in differentiating 
between NMO and MS as well as for its predictive value after 
an acute attack of TM. In one retrospective study, 55% of 
patients presenting with TM who were seropositive for anti-
AQP4-Ab experienced a second demyelinating event of either 
recurrent TM or optic neuritis during the next year.
257 In  
addition, AQP4-Ab–positive NMO is more frequently associ -
ated with another autoimmune disease, such as myasthenia 
gravis, systemic lupus erythematosus, Sjögren syndrome, and 
sarcoidosis.
Vascular Causes of Spinal Cord Injury
Ischemia of the spinal cord, although less common than isch -
emia of the brain, is a well-known cause of SCI. It is most com-
monly associated with the anterior cord syndrome and can  
occur as the result of systemic or local spinal cord hypoperfu -
sion, embolization, or (rarely) thrombosis. Ischemia can also 
result from the presence of a type I spinal arteriovenous malfor -
mation (AVM). This is a dural arteriovenous fistula that arises 
when a single dural arterial feeder, either spontaneously or after 
trauma, develops a fistula to the spinal venous circulation. This 
fistula causes venous congestion and hypertension, which results 
in hypoperfusion of the spinal cord. Symptoms caused by type 
I AVMs are usually of gradual onset with a progressive course, 
although there can be stepwise episodes of deterioration inter-
spersed with periods of clinical stability. Sensory symptoms are 
the most common initial presenting symptoms, but weakness 
and sphincter disturbances are often present by the time the 
diagnosis has been made.
139
Outcomes of Traumatic Spinal Cord Injury
Clinicians commonly hear from patients with traumatic SCI 
questions such as: “Will I walk again?” “Will I regain use of my 
hands?” “Will I regain control of my bowel and bladder?” Prog -
nosticating neurological and functional outcomes as early as pos -
sible facilitates developing a specific treatment plan and allows 
psychological adjustment to begin. Neurological prognosis de -
pends on the physical examination findings, especially as defined 
by the ISNCSCI. Younger age generally is more favorable for 
outcomes of ambulatory independence and self-care. ISNCSCI 
exams done between 24 hours and 1 week postinjury are the most 
reliable and predictive of neurological outcomes. A meta-analysis 
of 1162 patients did not find AIS grade conversion rates signifi -
cantly different between those who underwent ISNCSCI exam 
within 72 hours postinjury and after 72 hours postinjury.
84 Stud-
ies evaluating age as a factor when predicting outcomes after SCI 
are generally consistent in concluding that older persons (typi -
cally, a cohort age .50 years) have poorer improvement in func -
tion than younger patients.
146
Neurological Recovery in Complete Tetraplegia
After traumatic cervical SCI, approximately 30% of persons clas -
sified as AIS A convert to AIS B or better within the first year. This 
has been consistent across several independent large database 
analyses.
170,229 Within the Model Systems database, of those ini-
tially classified as AIS A, 8% convert to AIS C and 7% convert to 
AIS D. Of those initially classified as AIS B, 30% become AIS C 
while 37% convert to AIS D. Of those initially classified as AIS 
C, over 80% convert to AIS D or E. Of those who convert from 
AIS A to AIS B or greater, up to two-thirds convert by 1 month, 
with the remainder converting within 3 months. It is rare for a 
patient to convert later than that.
The upper extremity motor score (UEMS) is the sum of grades 
for the key muscles in the upper extremities and is a maximum of 
50. An analysis of SCI Model System data found a mean improve-
ment of 13.1 points in the UEMS and 9.7 points in the lower ex-
tremity motor score (LEMS) after 1 year. Those starting with AIS 
C show the best change in UEMS and LEMS compared with those

Studies analyzing the trend over time of conversion from com-
plete to incomplete injury have mixed results: Some report a 
higher rate of conversion in more recent articles,

study analyzing Sygen and EMSCI databases did not find a tem -
poral trend.229 Most motor recovery occurs in the first 3 months 
postinjury, then slows to a plateau by 12 to 18 months postinjury. 
Of patients with complete tetraplegia, 60% to 70% will gain one 
or more motor levels, but the likelihood of return of antigravity 
strength diminishes with a myotome’s distance inferiorly to the 
motor level. There is some recovery of sensory impairment after 
complete tetraplegia; analysis of EMSCI data demonstrated a 
mean improvement of 5.4 points for PP and 13 points for LT 
sensation.

Neurological Recovery in Paraplegia
Approximately 15% to 20% of patients with complete paraplegia 
will convert to incomplete paraplegia, and prognosis improves 
with more inferior level injuries. Neurological level of impairment 
in a thoracic level is determined by sensory testing. Among para -
plegic injuries, those at lumbar levels show the best prognosis for 
regaining strength in the lower extremity muscles. LEMS im-
provement after thoracic injuries is worse; T2–T6 injuries have 
worse outcomes than those at T7–T12. In patients with AIS B

Neurological Recovery and Ambulation
Using data from EMSCI, a combination of five variables—age 
(,65 vs. $65 years old), the motor score of the stronger quadri -
ceps femoris muscle, the motor score of the stronger gastrocsoleus 
complex, and LT sensation at the L3 and S1 dermatomes—show 
excellent discrimination in distinguishing persons who will be 
able to walk and those who will not be able to walk at 1 year  
(Fig. 50.16; Table 50.4). This algorithm was validated and modi-
fied by Hicks et al.
124 A simplified three-variable predictive model 
based on age and two neurological variables showed comparable 
accuracy for ambulation prognosis after traumatic SCI using the 
Canadian multicenter SCI database (the Rick Hansen Spinal 
Cord Injury Registry).
An even simpler clinical prediction rule for independent am -
bulation using the SCI Model System considers PP testing at the 
S1 dermatomes: Nine in 10 patients will report independent 
walking if they have normal bilateral PP sensation at the lateral 
heels, and 8 in 10 will report independent walking if there is any 
PP sensation in either lateral heel; whereas 8 in 10 patients are 
unable to walk if they lack bilateral PP sensation at S1.
220

motor strength seem to be major determinants of ambulation. In

Acute Phase of Injury
Prehospital Care
The first 24 hours after trauma have the highest mortality. In this 
period, primary and secondary injuries to the central nervous 
system are the leading cause of death. The first step in the treat -
ment of a person with a suspected SCI is ensuring an adequate 
airway, breathing, and circulation. Patients with cervical SCI are 
at high risk for respiratory failure and must be monitored closely 
for the need for ventilatory support. Even if intubation is not 
needed urgently, arterial blood gas and vital capacity (VC) mea -
surements are useful in identifying delayed respiratory muscle fa-
tigue. When intubation is necessary, it must be done carefully in 
the setting of suspected or confirmed cervical spine trauma to 
avoid secondary cord injury. The standard technique for urgent 
intubation in this setting is rapid sequence induction with cricoid 
pressure and manual inline stabilization.
55 Alternatively, awake 
fiberoptic intubation is an appropriate alternative and may be the 
preferred technique in a cooperative patient.
All persons with suspected acute SCI should have their spines 
immobilized. Persons with altered mental status, evidence of in -
toxication, suspected limb fracture or distracting injury, focal 
neurological deficit, and spine pain or tenderness should also be 
suspected of having a traumatic SCI. The entire spine should be 
immobilized in a neutral supine position regardless of the position 
in which the individual was found after the accident. This is best 
accomplished with the use of a combination of a rigid cervical 
collar with supportive blocks on a backboard that has straps to 
secure the body. On the spine board, an occipital pad for an adult 
or an occipital recess for a child younger than 6 years can be used 
to compensate for the different sizes of the head relative to the 
body in persons of different ages.
Once on the spine board, the individual is transported to a 
trauma center, where the initial goals are to establish hemody -
namic stability, to prevent hypoxemia and aspiration of stomach 
contents, and to maintain spinal immobilization until definitive 
management is accomplished. Maintenance of adequate blood 
pressure is critical because hypotension and shock are extremely 
deleterious to the injured spinal cord. A target mean arterial blood 
pressure (MAP) of 85 to 90 mm Hg for 5 to 7 days has been as -
sociated with favorable outcomes.
45,60,269 Maintaining MAP at a 
goal of 85 to 90 mm Hg is particularly important for those per -
sons with AIS A, B, or C. 45 Studies have suggested that falling 
below a MAP of 85 mm Hg for more than 2 hours can negatively 
affect neurological recovery.
60 Thus, it is vitally important to 
maintain MAP from time of injury through acute management, 
surgical management, and the entirety of the first week postinjury. 
The use of vasopressors may be necessary to do so. Spinal cord 
perfusion pressure is the difference between MAP and the CSF 
pressure. It is measured via lumbar intrathecal catheter, and studies
