outcome than MAP . Spinal cord perfusion pressures above  
50 mm Hg have been shown to support improved neurological 
recovery after SCI.227
Although neurogenic shock is associated with cervical and high 
thoracic injuries, it is important to evaluate the patient fully for 
other causes of shock, including hemorrhage, pneumothorax, myo-
cardial infarction, cardiac tamponade, sepsis from intraabdominal 
injury, or even acute adrenal insufficiency in patients with  
concomitant brain injury. Neurogenic shock is the result of sympa-
thetic denervation and is characterized by hypotension and brady-
cardia in the setting of flaccid paralysis. Bradycardia results from 
unopposed parasympathetic input to the heart, but it can also be 
stimulated by endotracheal suctioning. Neurogenic shock is treated 
with restoration of intravascular volume and vasopressor agents. 
The ideal pressor agents have both alpha- and beta-adrenergic ac-
tions to counter the loss of sympathetic tone and provide chrono-
tropic support to the heart.55 Atropine is helpful to rapidly reverse 
the bradycardia. Insertion of a temporary or permanent cardiac 
pacemaker is rarely necessary. Hemorrhagic shock is treated by 
controlling bleeding and vigorous fluid resuscitation. Core tem -
perature should be monitored because persons with cervical or high 
thoracic injuries can become relatively poikilothermic because of 
disruption of the autonomic nervous system. Small studies have 
shown safe and promising results for neurological outcome with 
modest intravascular hypothermia as a neuroprotective strategy.72,159 
Placement of a nasogastric tube in the acute period is important to 
prevent emesis and aspiration of gastric contents, while an indwell-
ing transurethral bladder catheter will ensure adequate bladder 
drainage in a situation where urinary retention is the rule. Early 
contact between the receiving trauma center and a specialized SCI 
center should be established, with arrangements made for transfer 
to the SCI center once medical stability has been secured.
Once the patient has been stabilized medically, a thorough 
evaluation of the neurological status and spinal stability is per -
formed. The neurological status is determined using the ISNC -
SCI. Serial examinations should be performed to detect neuro -
logical deterioration or improvement, particularly in the first 3 
days after injury and after manipulation such as transport, closed 
reduction, or surgical treatment. Spinal stability is assessed for the 
entire spine, not just the clinically likely area of injury, because 
there is an approximately 20% chance of finding noncontiguous 
spinal fractures. Computed tomography (CT) imaging of the 
entire spine is recommended because of the lack of sensitivity of 
plain film protocols, particularly in the craniocervical region and 
at the cervicothoracic junction. MRI evaluation is essential for 
evaluating tissues other than bone, including the spinal cord, 
nerve roots, ligaments, and intervertebral discs, and should be 
performed to evaluate the area of a known or suspected SCI. MRI 
evaluation is important particularly for the identification of spinal 
pathology in persons with a neurological deficit not identified by 
CT and for those who are unconscious or obtunded.
SCIWORA, a diagnosis much more common in children than 
in adults, is directly related to the greater flexibility of the young 
spine. When fractures are present as a cause of the SCI in chil -
dren, multiple noncontiguous fractures are seen one-third of the 
time, significantly more often than in adults.26
Surgical Management
In the specific case of dislocation of the cervical spine, rapid 
closed reduction using skeletal traction remains a valid treatment 
option. Closed reduction of a cervical dislocation is performed by 
applying a series of increasing distracting forces through the long 
axis of the body by means of a two-point attachment to the skull 
with a tong device. This device is attached to a rope that passes 
through a pulley attached to a weight. When the obstruction to 
normal alignment is overcome with the applied distraction force 
and the spine is realigned (e.g., a jumped facet), the distracting 
forces are reduced again. Use of tongs for traction in children has 
been associated with dural leaks; halo fixators are generally used 
instead, with an increased number of pins (8–10 as compared 
with 4 for adults) and lower halo pin torques than are used for 
adults.26 Surgery is performed after closed reduction to reestablish 
spinal stability.
Operative treatment of acute spinal injury is generally per -
formed either to stabilize an unstable spine or to decompress 
compressed neural elements, such as the spinal cord itself or nerve 
roots. Surgical decompression and intervention within 24 hours 
of SCI have been associated with threefold higher odds of neuro -
logical recovery, which is defined by at least a two-grade AIS  
improvement at 6 months postinjury.93 Moreover, surgical inter-
vention within 24 hours has been associated with decreased acute 
care and total hospital lengths of stay, improved cost efficiency, as 
well as fewer medical complications. 60,93,100,269 In general, the 
sooner decompression is achieved, the better the outcome. In fact, 
one study suggests that surgical decompression within 8 hours of 
SCI shows improved neurological recovery as compared with 
surgery within 24 hours of injury.158
If a traumatic SCI is not associated with spinal cord compres -
sion or spinal instability, surgery might not be indicated (e.g., in 
someone with SCIWORA). A cervical orthosis may be all that is 
indicated. Use of a spinal orthosis as the mainstay of management 
is also feasible for bony fractures, such as a compression fracture 
affecting only the anterior column of the spine without spinal 
cord compression.
Management of SCI often involves decompressive surgery.140 In 
particular, persons with incomplete injuries and spinal cord com-
pression may benefit from a decompressive procedure to maximize 
the potential for neurological recovery. Decompression of the spi-
nal cord in the setting of a neurologically complete injury has been 
more debated.154 It is well established that decompression of the 
cord in a complete injury is not usually associated with a change 
from complete to incomplete status. Therefore, the role of surgery 
in neurologically complete lesions is to provide early stability and 
rapid involvement of the injured person in rehabilitation, poten -
tially minimizing the occurrence of medical complications in the 
early phase of the postinjury period. Decompression of the cord in 
complete injuries may also reduce the incidence of posttraumatic 
cystic myelopathy and spinal cord tethering.
The surgical approach to the spine is determined based on the 
mechanism of injury, the location of spinal cord compression, 
and the surgeon’s expertise with the various surgical techniques. 
An anterior surgical approach provides the most direct decom -
pression of the spinal cord when compression of the cord is 
caused by retropulsion of bone fragments or disc material into 
the spinal canal. Anterior column reconstruction and restoration 
of spinal stability often can be accomplished using just an ante -
rior approach. There are, however, significant potential complica-
tions of an anterior approach. In the cervical region, surgical  
injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve can cause problems with 
speech and swallowing. Injury to the pharynx or esophagus can 
lead to mediastinitis or fistula formation to the trachea or skin. 
Because the anterior approach to the thoracic and upper lumbar

spine requires entry into the chest or retroperitoneum, there is a 
risk for respiratory complications and vascular injuries in addi -
tion to infectious complications. Moreover, several regions of the 
spine are inaccessible to repair via an anterior approach, includ -
ing the occipitocervical region, upper thoracic spine, and lower 
lumbar spine. A posterior approach is technically easier and less 
fraught with complications, although decompression of the spi -
nal cord may be indirect.
In addition to the use of stabilizing hardware, which may in -
clude combinations of plates, screws, cages, and rods, the spine 
surgeon uses bone grafts to ensure future spinal stability. That is, 
stabilization using instrumentation alone can fail over time, lead -
ing to instability and deformity. Bone used for spinal fusion can 
be autograft, obtained directly from the injured individual, or al -
lograft, obtained from a bone bank. Potential sites for autologous 
bone harvest include the iliac crest, the fractured spine itself, or a 
fibula. Spinal orthoses are commonly used to immobilize the 
spine after spinal fusion surgery to facilitate bone fusion. In most 
cases, orthoses are worn for 6 to 12 weeks postoperatively to allow 
for radiographically evident spinal fusion.
Penetrating injuries to the spinal cord are overwhelmingly 
caused by gunshot wounds. Stab wounds as a cause of SCI are 
relatively rare. Surgical management of such injuries is only rarely 
indicated. As a rule, neither of these mechanisms of injury causes 
spinal instability. Removal of bullets or bullet fragments is  
typically performed only if their presence in the spinal canal is 
associated with progressive neurological deterioration or there is a 
concern for delayed lead toxicity. Delayed lead toxicity may be 
more common for lead bullets in fluid cavities than in areas where 
they may be walled off and encapsulated by fibrous tissue.
Pharmacological Treatments
Currently, there is inadequate clinical evidence to recommend 
definitively any pharmacological agent to treat acute SCI. Meth -
ylprednisolone was studied in three large-scale studies known as 
the National Acute Spinal Cord Injury Study (NASCIS) trials and 
reportedly showed improvement in AIS motor and sensory scores; 
however, the studies suffered from confounders, and post hoc 
analysis did not reproduce the improvements in motor and sen -
sory scores. Once considered the standard of care, methylpred -
nisolone is no longer recommended.55
GM-1 ganglioside is a neuroprotective lipid that showed 
promise early in vitro and in vivo, but a large trial failed to show 
improvement in AIS grade, and a Cochrane review did not find 
any benefit. Gacyclidine, an N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor an -
tagonist, showed neuroprotective effects in animals, but a clinical 
trial with 200 participants did not find statistically significant 
improvement in neurological outcomes. Tirilazad and naloxone 
were studied as neuroprotective agents in NASCIS trials but did 
not show significant improvements.
A more recent phase III trial investigated riluzole, a sodium-
glutamate antagonist currently approved for treatment of amyo -
trophic lateral sclerosis; 193 participants with acute traumatic SCI 
were enrolled (54.4% of the target number) before the study was 
terminated because of the COVID-19 pandemic and left under -
powered. Motor scores and UEMS did not show improvement 
with riluzole, though all participants with cervical SCI who re -
ceived riluzole showed significant gain in functional recovery.92
Further research is needed before recommending riluzole’s use, 
though the authors highlight this study as an example of the dif -
ficulty in recruiting and conducting large-scale clinical trials in 
the SCI population, leading to negative findings so frequently. 
They suggest that regulatory bodies should consider more trials 
with innovative features, such as virtual controls,232 elimination of 
placebo groups, and higher regard for secondary outcomes, all of 
which could decrease overall cost, increase participation, and find 
benefits to new interventions.92
Cellular Treatments
Because motor and sensory recovery after traumatic SCI is often 
poor, there continues to be intense interest in finding an effective 
treatment for SCI. Historical studies suggested that the human 
central nervous system was not capable of regenerating axons after 
formation of a glial scar,129 a dense mesh layer of astrocytes, microg-
lia, and extracellular matrix proteins.207 Newer research in rodents 
showed this scar formation facilitates axon regeneration. 4 Experi-
mental evidence at the cellular level and from animal models has 
shown axonal regeneration and functional recovery through the use 
of synergistic therapies such as stem cells and growth factors.78
Many cellular categories have been studied preclinically and in 
human trials: Schwann cells, olfactory ensheathing cells (OECs), 
macrophages, hematopoietic and mesenchymal stem cells from 
bone marrow (BM-SCs), peripheral blood, adipose tissue, neural 
stem cells (NSCs), embryonic stem cells, and induced pluripotent 
stem cells. Ribeiro et al. 207 reviewed 131 articles reporting on a 
global effort to study cellular interventions in clinical trials  
(Fig. 50.17 ). More than 2500 participants received treatments 
(excluding control participants). China published the most nu -
merous studies (31) that together had enrolled 37.3% of all par -
ticipants in the review; 76.1% of the treated participants enrolled 
at least 6 months after injury.
The most notable adverse events came from transplantation of 
olfactory mucosa autografts (OMAs), after which some patients 
developed intramedullary masses of respiratory epithelium and 
submucosal glands where the OMAs were implanted. Therapies 
using support cells (i.e., Schwann cells and OECs) showed the 
best overall efficacy in AIS grades and scores. Studies using NSCs 
showed the best motor and sensory score improvements com -
pared with results using other stem cell types. All participants who 
received NSCs and BM-SCs together showed improvement in 
AIS grades and scores, suggesting that combined therapy may 
enhance the effect of NSCs.
Rehabilitation Phase of Injury
The Interdisciplinary Team
Rehabilitation goals after SCI include maximizing physical inde -
pendence, becoming independent in the direction of care, and 
preventing secondary complications. An interdisciplinary team 
approach is the model that has been used historically in the reha -
bilitation treatment of persons with SCI to achieve these goals. The 
team is optimally led by a physician who has obtained subspecialty 
board certification in SCI medicine and has undergone formal 
training in the interdisciplinary team approach. Other members of 
the team typically include the person with SCI, family members, 
physical therapists, occupational therapists, nurses, aides, dieti -
cians, psychologists, recreation therapists, vocational therapists, 
and social workers or case managers. Other consultant physicians, 
respiratory therapists, speech pathologists, clinician educators, or-
thotists, and driving instructors can also be members of the team 
depending on the specific injury and rehabilitation goals.

In the acute hospital setting, staff members who are not fully 
familiar with treating persons with SCI must be educated about 
the potential secondary complications of SCI and how to prevent 
them. The patient and family members must be educated about 
the nature of an SCI, the patient’s prognosis, and its uncertainty. 
T ransfer to a specialized SCI rehabilitation unit also should be 
facilitated because patients treated in such a unit have increased 
overall survival rates, decreased complication rates for pressure 
injuries and other problems, a decreased length of hospital stay, 
greater functional gains during rehabilitation, a greater likelihood 
of home discharge, and lower rehospitalization rates.
25,68 Physical 
and occupational therapists in the acute hospital should facilitate 
the prevention of secondary complications (e.g., contractures, 
pressure injuries, and disuse atrophy) through maintenance of 
joint ROM, splinting, positioning, and selective muscle strength-
ening. ROM of all joints is performed and taught by the thera -
pists to persons with SCI and their caregivers as soon as it is 
medically safe to do so. Performance of an adequate daily stretch-
ing program can prevent joint contractures. Splinting of joints, 
with either an off-the-shelf or a custom splint fabricated by an 
occupational therapist or orthotist, is also used often to provide a 
prolonged stretch, facilitate a functional joint position, and pre-
vent skin breakdown.
The inpatient rehabilitation setting is the cornerstone of the 
rehabilitation process for persons with SCI and seems to be es -
sential for attaining the already mentioned broad goals of SCI 
rehabilitation as well as to allow discharge from the hospital to the 
least restrictive possible setting, ideally to home. As the length of 
acute rehabilitation hospitalization decreases, many of the tasks 
described are further refined or even learned in an outpatient, 
skilled nursing facility or home setting. The rehabilitation process

in all its settings should empower individuals with SCI to know 
more than anyone else about their own bodies and to provide the 
resources to find solutions to all problems they might encounter 
in their daily activities and lives.
The person with SCI and family members are essential mem -
bers of the team. A patient who does not engage in the SCI  
rehabilitation program is not likely to experience much benefit. 
Rehabilitation nurses, in addition to performing their standard 
nursing duties, provide education on prevention and treatment of 
secondary complications as well as training in bowel and bladder 
management. Psychologists help to reduce depression and anxiety, 
as well as to facilitate adjustment to a catastrophic and life-altering 
injury by supporting persons with SCI (and their families) through 
the grieving process. This is achieved by providing individual psy-
chotherapy, cognitive behavioral techniques to enhance adaptive 
coping, and group psychotherapy to provide additional support 
and information sharing. Social workers or case managers help 
individuals with SCI, their families, and/or their caregivers to  
obtain needed available resources, benefits, and services. They fa -
cilitate the transition from an inpatient rehabilitation unit to a 
community/home setting or another facility and provide family 
support. Other physician consultants are typically involved at 
various points in the rehabilitation process, especially if secondary 
complications develop. Speech therapists evaluate and treat the 
swallowing and communicating problems that are common in 
individuals with tracheostomies, high cervical NLIs, and anterior 
approach cervical spinal surgeries. They commonly perform bed-
side swallowing evaluations and participate in modified barium 
swallow tests. They may also be needed to assess and treat cognitive 
dysfunction in the setting of individuals with concomitant brain 
injury that might have occurred along with the SCI.

Physical Skill Training
Physical and occupational therapists train persons with SCI in 
mobility, self-care skills, and other activities of daily living (ADL). 
The achievement of adequate joint ROM and strength necessary 
to exercise these skills are facilitated through ROM exercises, fab-
rication and use of appropriate orthoses, and progressive resis -
tance exercises. Individuals whose injuries prevent them from 
being independent without assistance must also be educated in 
how to direct caregivers to provide needed assistance. The person 
with SCI should be able to instruct caregivers on how to deliver 
the needed care in a safe and efficient manner. This is important 
to prevent injury both to the person with the SCI and caregivers.
T raining in activities performed on a therapy mat is commonly 
initiated as soon as a patient is able to tolerate being out of bed. 
These activities, often composed of separately performed parts of 
a more complex functional skill, are typically sequenced from the 
easiest to the most difficult. In progressing through these gradu -
ated skills, persons with SCI—who are often able to do little for 
themselves initially—move to a level of stability within a specific 
training posture. Finally, they are able to move in a safe and effec-
tive fashion to complete functional tasks. 212 Once the tasks are 
mastered on the mat, they are performed in other more real-life 
environments, such as in bed.
Mat activities include rolling, prone-on-elbows positioning, 
prone-on-hands positioning, supine-on-elbows positioning, long 
sitting, short sitting, quadruped positioning, and transfer train -
ing. First learning to roll on a mat, individuals with SCI rhythmi-
cally move their clasped outstretched arms from side to side while 
lying flat on their backs and then forcefully throw their out -
stretched arms to the side to which they are rolling. Rolling is 
facilitated by starting a roll from a semi–side-lying position with 
a pillow under one side of the back or with crossed legs.
Supine-on-elbows positioning can facilitate supine to a long 
sitting position. It can also help strengthen shoulder extensor and 
adductor musculature. First, they place their hands under their 
hips; next, they flex their elbows; and finally, they shift their 
weight from side to side to maneuver their elbows underneath 
their upper body. Mat sitting begins with balance exercises either 
in a long sitting position with the legs extended on the mat or in 
a short sitting position with the knees bent at a 90-degree angle 
and resting on the ground. Sitting pushups are taught to facilitate 
moving about the mat and making transfers.
T ransfer training should be taught in accordance with tech -
niques that minimize stress on an individual’s joints and muscles, 
facilitate energy economy, and maximize safety. This can be  
accomplished by encouraging the use of a validated transfer as -
sessment instrument by all teachers of transfers. 264 For a person 
with complete paraplegia or lower tetraplegia, the first transfer 
usually taught is one that uses a transfer board. For a transfer into 
or out of a manual wheelchair, the wheelchair is positioned at an 
angle of 30 to 45 degrees from a parallel position to the mat with 
the front of the seat nearest the mat. This allows clearance of the 
rear wheels by the buttocks during the transfer. For a transfer into 
or out of a power wheelchair, the wheelchair is positioned parallel 
to the mat because the high wheels on a manual wheelchair are 
not in the way on a power chair. Next, the individual scoots for -
ward in the chair, removes the armrest, inserts the transfer board 
deep under the leg closest to the mat, and then rocks head and 
shoulders away from the mat while simultaneously pushing up 
and toward the mat with the arm farthest from the mat. This 
causes the buttocks to move onto the transfer board. The rocking 
and pushing process is repeated until the individual is safely on 
the mat, at which time the transfer board is removed. Leg rests 
may have to be removed for the transfer. A popover transfer is 
similar except that a transfer board is not used.
In addition to these techniques, several other types of transfer 
techniques are used by persons with varying levels of neurological 
function. These include the dependent lift transfer, mechanical 
lift transfer, stand-pivot transfer, sit-pivot transfer, and floor-to-
chair transfer. The mechanical lift transfer uses a mechanical  
device attached to a sling. The dependent lift transfer and me -
chanical lift transfer are used mainly for individuals who are unable 
to physically assist in the transfers. The stand-pivot and sit-pivot 
transfers require weight bearing on the lower limbs and are useful 
only if a person has significant lower extremity extensor tone or 
adequate lower extremity strength to squat or stand briefly. The 
floor-to-chair transfer is important for anyone who falls out of a 
wheelchair or otherwise ends up on the floor and must get back 
into the chair or onto another higher surface. Standing can be 
initiated on a standing frame, a tilt table, in a standing wheelchair, 
or with an exoskeletal device. Standing seems to help lessen bone 
loss after an acute injury; it also improves physical self-concept and 
self-reported health.30
Standing should be implemented only with caution in indi -
viduals with chronic SCI in consideration of osteoporosis. Indi -
viduals with osteoporosis have a risk for fracture even without 
weight bearing (see Musculoskeletal Conditions, later). Although 
ambulation is an expressed goal of most people who have experi -
enced an SCI, recovery of ambulation is variable. For persons 
with incomplete motor SCI, gait training can be facilitated by 
body weight support (BWS), although BWS training has not been 
shown to be superior to conventional gait training for ambula -
tion.76 For persons with complete thoracic-level injuries who wish 
to undergo ambulation training, orthoses that stabilize the knees 
and ankles are required. A swing-through gait pattern (Fig. 50.18) 
is taught in several steps similar to the mat activities already de -
scribed. This begins in the parallel bars and includes going from 
sit to stand, balancing with extended hips, pushups in the stand -
ing position, turning while standing, recovery from a flexed-hip 
position, advancement of the lower extremities with hip hiking, 
performance of a step-to gait, and finally a swing-through-gait 
pattern. After the swing-to or swing-through-gait pattern is mas -
tered in the parallel bars, it is performed with a walker or crutches.
The use of exoskeletons for ambulation for persons who have 
some truncal stability and the use of their arms to allow the use of 
upper extremity assistive devices such as a walker or forearm 
crutches is beginning to be favored in clinical settings in the 
United States and around the world, including in the acute inpa-
tient rehabilitation setting. These devices can allow upright walk-
ing in persons who do not have adequate lower extremity strength 
to allow walking with or without conventional bracing with only 
minimal to moderate energy expenditure (see Exoskeletons, later).
Wheelchair Skills
Physical and occupational therapists not only train persons with 
SCI in wheelchair mobility but also help to select the proper 
seating systems to ensure proper sitting position. Wheelchair us -
ers are taught to manage or direct all wheelchair components, 
including the brakes, arm rests, foot rests, wheels, and seat cush -
ion. They are taught how to fold or break down the chair so that 
it can be placed properly in a vehicle. They are taught wheelchair 
propulsion, first indoors over level surfaces and then outdoors

over uneven terrain. Proper body mechanics are taught to achieve 
efficient wheelchair propulsion patterns, including an ideal pro -
pulsive stroke and an ideal recovery stroke. An ideal propulsive 
stroke is one that occurs at a steady speed that maximizes  
the hand rim “contact” and “push” angle (angle along the arc of 
the pushrim) while keeping stroke frequency and forces to a 
minimum. Although self-selected wheelchair propulsion patterns 
are often not the most efficient ones, experienced wheelchair us -
ers often significantly improve propulsion biomechanics from 
early to late during extended propulsion. Individually selected 
wheelchair propulsion patterns are often influenced by poor 
wheelchair sitting positions. Of several propulsion patterns that 
have been described, differing primarily in their recovery phase, 
a semicircular wheelchair propulsion pattern has been shown to 
be the most efficient and least stressful on the shoulders and 
nerves crossing the wrist.
31 Another basic wheelchair mobility 
skill is the performance of a wheelie, in which the individual in 
the wheelchair balances on the rear two wheels. This is an impor-
tant skill that must be mastered to become independent in curb 
and single-step climbing in a wheelchair.
Spinal Cord Injury Education
During the early phases of SCI, most patients and their families 
have little knowledge or understanding of the injury; its multiple 
consequences; the myriad of interventional and management op-
tions, community resources, and equipment needs; and the prog-
nosis for life, health, and function. They are often overwhelmed 
by the gravity of the situation and unable to adjust to an altered 
lifestyle and self-image. A comprehensive education program is an 
essential part of any SCI rehabilitation program, and if properly 
designed, helps the person with SCI and the family members not 
only to gain knowledge but also to adjust and prepare emotionally 
for a successful community reintegration. Although some of the 
learning occurs in formal education classes or group discussions, 
from educational materials published by various SCI organiza -
tions, or educational videos on the internet, one-on-one instruc -
tion by health care professionals is thought to be the most helpful 
in addressing individual needs and concerns. Nevertheless, when 
implementing an educational program, multiple formats of edu -
cation should be provided to facilitate learning because different 
people respond more or less to different methods of learning, and 
this allows for repetition. With proper education and the ability 
to access appropriate information readily, the person with SCI 
becomes best able to manage the various impairments successfully 
and to ensure the highest possible function and quality of life. 
With regard to published educational materials, there are several 
well-regarded consumer-oriented resource guides that are freely 
available online, including Yes, You Can! A Guide to Self-Care for 
Persons with Spinal Cord Injury, published by the PVA.
38 There are 
also the What You Should Know Consumer Guides series published 
by the PVA,252 and the Paralysis Resource Guide published by the 
Christopher and Dana Reeve Foundation.197
The curriculum of a structured SCI education program should 
be as broad as possible and should include the anatomy, physiol -
ogy, and classification of SCI as well as its various medical conse -
quences, the psychosocial adjustments that must be made, its  
effects on sexual health and fertility, assistive technology available, 
unique nutritional needs, available community resources, and 
ongoing research to improve neurological function.
Home and Environmental Modifications
Helping a person with SCI regain mobility with a wheelchair is of 
little use if the architecture at home, school, work, and in the com-
munity prevents entry by wheelchair or mobility once inside.  
Inaccessible environments jeopardize an individual’s dignity,  
self-sufficiency, and quality of life. The Specially Adapted Housing 
Act of 1948 provided grant assistance to veterans with service-  
connected disability to obtain special wheelchair-accessible

housing. In 1990, the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) ex-
panded the rights of people with disabilities, including prohibiting 
discrimination against them. It also required removal of architec -
tural barriers in facilities owned by organizations that receive fed-
eral funding to give persons with disabilities equal access to these 
facilities.
The ADA does not demand removal of architectural barriers in 
private homes, many of which remain inaccessible for wheelchair 
users. A home evaluation, which can be defined as a review of the 
details of a home environment with attention to barriers to access, 
is best performed before a new wheelchair user returns home. A 
home evaluation typically includes provision of a floor plan—  
either hand drawn, captured on a floor plan–making mobile  
application, or professionally completed—with annotated mea -
surements of all thresholds, room sizes, doorway widths, furni -
ture, appliance and plumbing placements, and elevations such as 
stairs and ramps to which the individual returning home will have 
access. It may be possible to complete this with a questionnaire 
outlining these details or through pictures or videos describing the 
same information. Based on the information within the floor plan 
(which is typically provided by a family member or someone who 
has access to the residence or even sometimes by a therapist or 
other contracted individual), the individual with SCI will choose 
in close collaboration with the therapists what specific durable 
medical equipment (DME) is needed to return home safely. 
DME is then ordered and ideally delivered before discharge. De -
pending on the setup of the home, there may have to be architec-
tural changes, and contracting with architects and builders will 
have to be initiated by the individual or caregivers.
Driver Training
Being able to drive an automobile enhances the mobility and 
quality of life of persons with SCI. Most people with SCI can 
drive an automobile with the proper adaptive equipment and 
training. Generally, persons with C1–C4 neurological levels from 
traumatic SCI and those with other severe impairments are un -
able to drive. People with paraplegia can usually drive with basic 
hand controls for acceleration and braking, and most are capable 
of transferring between the driver’s seat and the wheelchair inde-
pendently and of loading the chair into the car. People with  
tetraplegia usually choose to drive a modified van with a wheel -
chair lift or ramp and varying degrees of modification of the 
control mechanisms.
Some people with C5 tetraplegia can drive 1 year after their 
injury. Typically, they use a power wheelchair for mobility and are 
not able to transfer between the wheelchair and car. They require 
a van with power door openers, an automatic lift or ramp, and 
extensive modifications of the control mechanisms. Occasionally, 
they require a multiaccess driving system in which the steering, 
accelerator, and brake are operated by a single control lever.
The driving skills of people with C6 tetraplegia vary consider -
ably, but most are able to turn the steering wheel. Many are not 
capable of operating regular hand control mechanisms and  
require powered or electronic hand controls for braking and ac -
celeration. Most people with C7–C8 tetraplegia have enough 
upper limb strength to operate a standard steering wheel with a 
terminal device. The terminal device compensates for their poor 
hand grip; examples include a knob, cuff, tri-pin, or special grip.
Over the years, major advances in assistive driving technology 
have made it possible for more people with disabilities to operate 
a vehicle safely. Steering and braking can be managed with one 
hand through a tri-pin or joystick terminal device. This can incor-
porate the secondary controllers (e.g., gear shift, turn signals, 
hazard warning lights, horn, dimmers, cruise control, washer, 
wipers, radio, air conditioner, heater, defroster, doors, lift, steering 
tilt). All drivers must use seat belts, but those with reduced trunk 
control must also use safety belts to secure trunk stability, such as 
shoulder, chest, or lap belts.
All persons with disability wanting to drive should undergo a 
driving evaluation by a specialist, usually an occupational thera -
pist certified by the Association of Driver Educators for the Dis -
abled (ADED). The ADED website can help to locate certified 
driving evaluators in the United States and Canada. A predriving 
evaluation includes an assessment of the person’s medical and 
driving history as well as functional capabilities. Interactive driv -
ing simulators can present the users with diverse challenges in a 
safe environment and can provide objective measurements of 
driving behaviors, but an on-the-road driving evaluation is essen-
tial. Selection of the proper vehicle and appropriate modifications 
to fit the user’s ability involves input from various members of the 
rehabilitation team. The driving educator ensures that all installed 
equipment is appropriate and that the driver is able to use the 
controls. Behind-the-wheel training by the driving educator can 
be a lengthy process for persons with high-level tetraplegia be -
cause of the complexity of the equipment and the impairment of 
the learner. The high cost of vehicles and modifications and the 
complexity of training are the reasons some people with high-level 
tetraplegia (C5–C6) choose not to drive.
Vocational Training
Employed persons who have SCI report higher levels of psycho -
logical adjustment, satisfaction with life, independence, and gen -
eral health than do those who are unemployed. Furthermore, 
their risk of mortality is lower. 151 Nevertheless, only about one-
quarter of all individuals with SCI are employed. Among persons 
listed in the National SCI Model System Database, approximately 
63% were employed at the time of their SCI, 19% were students, 
and 17% were unemployed. The relatively high number of unem-
ployed persons at the time of injury is recognized as a factor 
negatively affecting postinjury employment.150
Predictors of employment after SCI include being employed 
before SCI, having a less physically demanding occupation be -
fore SCI, being younger at the time of SCI, having a less severe 
SCI, having lived more years with SCI, having had more educa -
tion before SCI, being motivated to work, and being White. 150 
Education has been found to be the factor most strongly associ -
ated with postinjury employment. Only 5% of persons in the 
National SCI Model System Database with less than 12 years of 
education were found to be employed after SCI, but with each 
successive educational milestone completed, employment num -
bers improved, reaching a high of 70% employment of persons 
with doctoral degrees. In general, by 10 years postinjury, one-
third of persons with paraplegia and one-quarter of those with 
tetraplegia are employed.
Vocational rehabilitation is concerned with supporting efforts 
by a person with a disability to return to and maintain employ -
ment. Rehabilitation counselors, who facilitate this process,  
usually have a master’s degrees in rehabilitation counseling and 
optimally are accredited by the Council on Rehabilitation Educa-
tion. Vocational rehabilitation typically begins with an evaluation 
of the person’s functional limitations, barriers to employment, 
transferable skills, career interests, and prior achievement. Skills

are assessed during simulated or actual work. Counseling and  
support with regard to educational or vocational reentry, job ac -
commodation, and supported employment typically follow. The 
rehabilitation counselor can liaise with employers who may have 
little experience interacting with persons with disability and can 
have difficulty imagining how a person with an SCI could per -
form a specific job. Job accommodations, or the modifications of 
a job to make it accessible to a person with SCI, may involve 
modification of the jobsite, use of adaptive equipment, or job 
policy changes. Modifications of the jobsite can range from the 
simple, such as adjustment of desk height to accommodate a 
wheelchair, to the complex, such as redesign of an assembly line. 
Adaptive equipment can include tools with special handles or sit-
stand workstations. Job policy changes can include reassignment 
of physical tasks or changes in the number and length of workday 
breaks. Supported employment refers to the need by the individ -
ual with SCI for additional assistance in the workplace, ranging 
from a need for a full-time personal care assistant to the occasional 
need for help in manipulating work materials.
Several legislative initiatives have been implemented with the 
goal of promoting employment of persons with disabilities. The 
Rehabilitation Act of 1973 provides federal funding for vocational 
rehabilitation programs in each state. The ADA attempts to pre -
vent discrimination in employment against qualified individuals 
who are able to perform essential functions of a job with or with-
out accommodation.3 Workers’ compensation programs in most 
states provide a vocational rehabilitation program for workers 
who are injured at work, including retraining of those who cannot 
return to a previous or similar job.
Reconstructive Surgery of the Upper Limbs
There are two broad categories of reconstructive surgical ap -
proaches to improve function after SCI: tendon transfers and 
nerve transfers. T endon transfer refers to (1) the detachment of a 
tendon of an expendable innervated muscle from one of its at -
tachments and (2) the reattachment of the innervated muscle and 
tendon to another tendon that lacks an innervated muscle but 
whose regained function is sought. T echniques for tendon trans-
fers are well established and produce generally predictable out -
comes (see later), but after the procedure, new patterns of muscle 
activation must be learned and trained. Alternatively, nerve trans-
fers are becoming more widely used because the anatomical 
muscle designed for a specific function is reinnervated, thus 
avoiding the complications, technical issues, and intensive func -
tional rehabilitation required after tendon transfers. The periph -
eral nerve to be used must be intact, and its response to stimula -
tion is tested intraoperatively. Both tendon and nerve transfers 
can be planned for the same case (though not to the same target 
muscle) to provide the most functional limb.
T enodesis action refers to the passive tightening of a tendon 
when stretched by the movement over a proximal joint over which 
it crosses, causing movement of a distal joint. A passive tenodesis 
hand is one in which there is no volitional control of intrinsic or 
extrinsic hand muscles or the wrist extensors. Opening and closing 
of the hand can only occur with passive tenodesis action through 
forearm pronation and supination, respectively. An active tenode-
sis hand is one in which there is the addition of active wrist exten-
sion, which allows for passive movements of the fingers into a 
grasp dependent on tenodesis action; relaxation of the wrist  
extension allows for release. Grasp caused by tenodesis action is 
usually more effective in a hand that has developed tightness  
of paralyzed muscles to achieve a functional grasp position. It can 
be accentuated through the reconstructive surgical procedure de -
scribed later. An active extrinsic hand is one with voluntary con -
trol of wrist extensors and extrinsic finger flexors that can allow 
some grasp with or without tenodesis action, whereas an active 
extrinsic-intrinsic hand is one in which there is volitional control 
of both the intrinsic and extrinsic hand musculature.  
Arthrodesis refers to joint fusion whereby the joint cartilage is re -
moved from either side of the joint, and the exposed bony ends 
are opposed and allowed to fuse. T enodesis alone refers to the 
surgical attachment of a tendon to a bone.
Functional upper limb surgical reconstruction has historically 
been delayed for 1 year postinjury to allow for neurological recovery 
in targeted muscles. Muscles are chosen for transfer if they have a 
strength grade of 4 or 5 because one grade of muscle strength is usu-
ally lost with the transfer. T ransferred muscles with a strength grade 
less than 3 generally do not improve function. Muscles should not 
be chosen for transfer if their loss would result in a functional de-
cline. After a tendon transfer procedure, the tendon constructs are 
typically immobilized for several weeks in a nonstretched position, 
followed by gradual mobilization, strengthening, and reeducation. 
Functional electrical stimulation (FES) is often used to facilitate 
neuromuscular reeducation. Common tendon transfers and tenode-
sis procedures are shown in Table 50.6, stratified to ISNCSCI motor 
levels and the more specific International Classification for Surgery 
of the Hand in T etraplegia motor groups.172
Functional improvements achieved after a deltoid or biceps-  
to-triceps transfer primarily occur through an increased ability to 
stabilize the arm and reach overhead. Improvements can be seen in 
feeding, grooming, pressure relief performance, and writing. Func-
tional improvements after a transfer of the brachioradialis (BR) to 
the extensor carpi radialis brevis can include an increased ability to 
pick up objects, feed, groom, write, and type. Functional improve-
ments after a transfer of the BR to the flexor pollicis longus (FPL) 
can include an increased ability to pick up a pen and write, more 
efficient grooming, and less dependence on a wrist-hand orthosis 
for grasp. The combination of transfers of the BR to the FPL and 
the extensor carpi radialis longus to the flexor digitorum profun -
dus (FDP) tendon has been shown to lead to improved key pinch, 
grasp strength, and subjective ADL performance. Functional im -
provements after the combination of transfers of the BR to the 
FPL and of the pronator teres to the FDP tendon have been noted 
in manual wheelchair propulsion, lower limb dressing, opening of 
jars, and the transferring of objects with the reconstructed hands.
The three most commonly reported nerve transfer techniques 
include transfer of the posterior division of the axillary nerve 
(teres minor and posterior deltoid nerve branches) to the triceps 
to provide elbow extension, the supinator nerve branch to the 
posterior interosseous nerve to provide thumb and finger exten -
sion, and the brachialis branch of the musculocutaneous nerve to 
the anterior interosseous nerve fascicle of the median nerve to 
restore median nerve function and provide pronation, wrist flex -
ion, and finger flexion of the thumb and the index and middle 
fingers.40 Data on the functional improvements resulting from 
these nerve transfer techniques come mainly from case reports.
Functional Electrical Stimulation for 
Therapeutic Exercise
Sedentary lifestyle and impaired autonomic function in persons 
with SCI can lead to many degenerative physiological changes

ance, and bone density are diminished in the paralyzed limbs; 
cardiovascular fitness, VC, and lean body mass are reduced; and 
endocrine functions are altered. FES-induced leg exercise, 
achieved most commonly on cycle ergometers—but also with 
rowing, poling, stepping, and other repetitive exercise machines—
has been shown to improve cardiorespiratory fitness, lower ex
-
tremity circulation, and exercise capacity; it also increases muscle 
size, decreases pain, and alters bone mineral density (BMD).
62 
During arm exercise with concurrent FES cycling or FES rowing, 
oxygen uptake is higher than with arm or leg exercise alone. This 
increase in oxygen uptake is not associated with an increase in 
perceived exertion. FES cycling (both stationary and overground) 
and FES rowing have become popular competitive sports.
FES is not effective in stimulating muscles paralyzed by LMN 
damage (i.e., damage affecting the anterior horn cells, motor 
nerve roots, or both), but such damage usually occurs at the level 
of the SCI lesion. FES is usually a relatively safe intervention, but 
its application in the presence of cardiac pacemakers or implanted 
defibrillators is best avoided. Caution is also warranted in the 
presence of various heart conditions (especially dysrhythmia and 
congestive heart failure) and during pregnancy.
Body Weight–Supported Ambulation 
Training on a Treadmill
Specific intensive walking training in persons with incomplete 
SCI significantly improves walking ability. T raining consists of 
upright walking on a treadmill with partial BWS provided by a 
suspending harness, and a therapist guiding and setting the limbs. 
BWS training has been shown to be effective in improving ambu-
latory function after SCI, although not more effectively than 
equivalent overground mobility training.
76
Exoskeletons
Exoskeletons are robotic systems worn by humans to “augment 
strength and endurance.”
110 The first exoskeleton patent filed in 
1917 described a series of wired “ligaments” driven by steam-
powered pistons.
142 An early modern prototype borne out of a  
US Department of Defense contract in the late 1960s weighed 
1500 pounds and was designed for heavy-duty industrial use for 
soldiers (Hardiman prototype). Current exoskeletons, US Food 
and Drug Administration (FDA)–approved for rehabilitation, are 
more advanced technologically but share a similar fundamental

design: a rigid frame to support the human body, joints approxi -
mately aligned with those of the wearer, and servo motors and/or 
hydraulic actuators to exert movements. A lightweight (29-pound) 
exoskeleton has a modular design donned by connecting five 
pieces around the user. Others are donned by securing straps and 
braces in the sitting position.
Exoskeleton-Assisted Walking
Exoskeleton-assisted walking (EAW) provides guided locomotion 
with moderate energy expenditure for individuals with volitional 
upper extremity motor function sufficient to support themselves 
with a walker or other bilateral upper extremity ambulatory assis-
tive devices. Powered steps are generally triggered by the upright 
user leaning toward an expected vector (e.g., hip tilt, or lateral 
plus forward shift, or forward tilt) ( Video 50.1). Certain modes 
can provide manual triggering of steps and variable powered sup-
port. T raining protocols vary significantly in number of weeks per 
program, minutes per session, and frequency of sessions. EAW has 
demonstrated feasibility in inpatient and outpatient settings for 
individuals with paraplegia and lower levels of tetraplegia. 122
Clinical Benefits
The majority of the literature related to EAW consists of small pilot 
studies and case reports, which suggest positive clinical trends 
overall. In a study of seven exoskeleton users with SCI, most in -
creased their functional ability with training (after 18 sessions) 
such that only contact guard or supervision assistance was needed 
for walking and sit-to-stand transfers. Another showed that steps 
increased from 59 to 2284 per session after 10 to 15 weeks of 
training.114 Mean gait speeds among the three FDA-approved 
exoskeletons are 0.28, 0.14, and 0.36 m/s,114 all of which are sig-
nificantly less than the “full community ambulation” minimum of 
0.8 m/s. A small study showed 7 of 12 participants with SCI per-
formed EAW at greater than 0.4 m/s, the minimum to be consid-
ered partial community ambulation.265 The average speed needed 
to safely cross a street is 1.06 m/s.46
Fat mass, measured with total body dual-energy x-ray absorp -
tiometry, decreased after 54 to 76 sessions of EAW 11 ( n 5  8). 
EAW showed improved seated balance as measured by seated 
posturography after an EAW training program 241 (n 5  8). Par -
ticipants with motor-complete paraplegia have shown greater 
electromyographic activity of trunk muscles during EAW com -
pared with robotic body weight–supported treadmill training 
(BWSTT)1 ( n 5  8). In multiple small studies, spasticity was  
reduced after EAW sessions based on objective and subjective 
measures.178 Neuropathic pain was statistically reduced after EAW 
among 12 participants with baseline pain. 228
Multiple studies have analyzed the effects of EAW on cardio -
vascular parameters (e.g., oxygen uptake, heart rate, blood pres -
sure, metabolic equivalent of task units) and suggest that EAW 
could be used for cardiovascular exercise depending on model 
and operating mode. 178 Various cardiometabolic studies show 
parameters consistent with light to moderate exercise during 
EAW: heart rate increases, rating of perceived exertion increases, 
and average VO 2 significantly higher during walking than at 
rest.12 Small survey studies suggest benefit on neurogenic bowel 
function with EAW , even outperforming BWSTT , thus implying 
a mechanism beyond upright walking activity. However, one 
study with six participants did not report benefit to bowel  
function. An electromyographic study showed a decrease in  
H-reflex/M-wave amplitude ratio after 10 weeks of EAW ,  
suggesting decreased hyperreflexia. 24
Limitations
Although the eventual goal of EAW is inherently a return to 
“walking” (or at least a goal of full community ambulation for 
sustained time and distance), the exoskeleton systems have not 
achieved this milestone yet. Small reports have shown increased 
function in number of steps and transfers after extended training, 
and others demonstrate positive effects on impairments caused by 
SCI, but all prospective research reviewed here involved small, 
pilot study sample sizes. T raining protocols that require tens of 
weeks of sessions, multiple times per week, are arduous for staff  
and participants alike. Perhaps with the advent of supportive in -
surance reimbursement models and FDA approvals for home use, 
larger studies can be done in a home environment. However, 
completing a full facility-based training program is required to 
bring an exoskeleton home for now, so work demands on staff are 
still significant.
Future Work
Envisioning industrial robotic arms articulating about multiple 
joints as they quickly and smoothly reach targets to weld or fasten, 
one may wonder why rehabilitation exoskeletons have not  
reproduced similar smooth movements toward a natural gait. 
Improving software with artificial intelligence algorithms for  
autonomous decision making could help systems respond better 
to the user and ground environment. Rigid ankle-foot orthoses 
integrated in current rehabilitation exoskeletons provide a stable 
base of support, but perhaps they can be replaced by soft actuators 
with dynamic rigidity to reproduce more natural plantarflexion. 
Rigid hip joints can offer basic reciprocating gait (in which the 
stance limb’s hip extends while swing limb’s hip flexes), but can an 
exoskeleton’s pelvic orthosis actuate complex movements to re -
semble more natural pelvic motion? Exoskeletons enhanced with 
FES applied to the lower extremity muscles have yielded faster 
walking speeds but at the cost of earlier muscular fatigue. In a 
2020 case report, combining spinal cord epidural stimulation 
(SCES) with EAW demonstrated decreasing the exoskeleton 
swing assistance from 100% to 35%, increased EAW speed, and 
improvement in EMG activity of the lower extremities.114 A sub-
sequent case study by the group documented two participants 
with motor complete injuries and percutaneous SCES enabling 
increased muscle activation and movement below the level of in -
jury and decreased assistance during EAW .115 Another group used 
a prototype research exoskeleton in conjunction with electroen -
cephalography decoded in real time to demonstrate exoskeletal 
control via a brain machine interface. 165
Soft Exoskeleton Designs
Although rigid, powered orthoses for ambulation may be the pro-
totypical concept of “rehabilitation exoskeleton,” there is much 
research in the use of soft actuators in flexible exoskeletal designs 
of devices that assist with other ADL. For example, a belt-shaped 
pneumatic artificial muscle (PAM), whose length shortens with 
inflation, could be used to tighten pants or doff them quickly.123 A 
soft, wearable insufflator-exsufflator device to assist with mucous 
clearance becomes rigid only at the time of operation by using a 
technique termed layer jamming—compliant layers of a frictional

material increase in stiffness when subjected to vacuum pressure.273 
Three-dimensional printed meshes can exhibit auxetic designs—
that is, the material expands transversely when stretched instead of 
thins—for application as peripheral joint bracing that is flexible 
and conforming in one direction but rigid and restrictive in the 
opposite direction.204 PAMs require a source of pressurized air, 
typically via a compressor connected externally by a hose. A novel, 
wearable battery-powered hydraulic motor has been designed with 
high torque and peak force far higher than the forces exerted by 
the hip, knee, and ankle during ambulation.71 The compact system 
weighs 1.6 kg and could serve as a practical means to integrate 
flexible actuators into exoskeleton design.
Exoskeletons to assist walking have demonstrated feasibility, 
and more research is needed to optimize hardware and software 
toward designs that offer sustained utility throughout each day. 
Integrating soft actuators into assistive exoskeletal devices is early 
in research but carries much promise as well.
Epidural Spinal Cord Stimulation
The use of neuromodulation, specifically epidural electrical stimu-
lation, of the spinal cord as a way to treat and improve function 
has gained momentum and seen promising results. In 2011, epi -
dural subthreshold motor stimulation to the lumbosacral spinal 
cord (using commercially available epidural stimulators such as 
those for the treatment of pain) in conjunction with extensive 
voluntary motor training in a person with chronic motor com -
plete SCI showed recovery of full weight bearing and independent 
standing.81 Subsequent work has shown effectiveness in using 
commercially available epidural stimulators and specially engi -
neered paddle stimulators in promoting volitional motor recovery 
and autonomic function in persons with SCI. 56,119
The work done in this space has elucidated several findings. 
Careful configurations of epidural stimulator leads, proper train -
ing, and advanced software are needed to achieve results of per -
sons standing and walking with SCI.119,208 However, these results 
have been reproduced with several individuals with complete SCI 
regaining the ability to stand and walk and as such are a promis -
ing treatment.119,141 This field of research has also led to the dis -
covery that both motor and autonomic improvements can be 
achieved with epidural stimulation and the discovery of previ -
ously unknown spinal networks, spinal interneurons, and growth 
factors necessary to achieve these results in persons with SCI.5,6,141
Most recently, a person with chronic tetraplegia was able to 
stand and walk using a brain-spine interface (BSI). 166 The BSI 
consists of not only an epidural electrical stimulation system but 
also a cortical recording device. 166 The BSI forms a connection 
between these cortical signals and the epidural stimulator allow -
ing for, after calibration and training, cortical control of stimula -
tion and subsequent movement. 166 The implanted individual  
reported a near natural control of standing and walking. 166 Con-
tinued evolution of epidural electrical stimulation technology and 
the ability to link this stimulation to rapidly evolving brain ma -
chine interface technology shows significant promise for recovery 
after SCI.
Chronic Phase of Injury
Adjustment to Disability
SCI is a catastrophic event with profound effects on the injured 
person as well as on family members. Many factors (e.g., personal, 
interpersonal, social, financial and cultural) ultimately determine 
how successfully an individual with SCI will adjust or adapt to the 
disability. Successful adjustment is thought to have occurred 
when the disability is no longer the dominant concern in the 
person’s life. The timeframe for such adjustment is highly variable 
but has been estimated by some to occur over 2 to 5 years, 
whereas others propose that adjustment is a lifelong process. Ad -
justment has been considered by many to consist of sequential 
psychological reactions according to stage theories, beginning 
with denial and followed by anger, bargaining, depression, and 
finally acceptance or adjustment. Although such sequential reac -
tions are often seen in persons with SCI, they tend to be highly 
individualized, with some people never seeming to pass though 
some of the stages and others being stuck in one stage forever.
Despite an overwhelming initial sense of loss, most persons 
with SCI eventually learn to cope emotionally and conform to 
their premorbid personality styles of interacting with the environ-
ment. A number of psychological problems, however, primarily 
depression and anxiety, can interfere with adjustment and quality 
of life. These problems can lead to substance abuse, divorce, de -
pendency, self-neglect, and even suicide.
The prevalence of major depression in persons with SCI at any 
given time is approximately 20%, of whom 15% have suicidal 
ideation.126 Interpersonal factors, such as perceived burdensome -
ness and pain, are risk factors for suicide after SCI. 145 Up to 7% 
of persons with SCI obtained their injury as a result of attempted 
suicide.143 These individuals and those with other premorbid 
psychiatric disorders are at higher risk for attempting suicide after 
SCI.143
The natural history of depression after SCI seems to be that for 
those evaluated at 5 years postinjury, half have chronic depression. 
Declining health, worsening pain, and unhealthy alcohol use are 
risk factors for developing major depression when one was not 
depressed earlier. Other risk factors for depression are similar to 
those in able-bodied individuals, especially a family history of 
depression. Factors specific to the SCI, such as the presence of 
complete neurological injury, poor self-perceived health, func -
tional impairments, and medical comorbidities, increase the 
likelihood of developing depression. As major depression is treat -
able and not intrinsic to the SCI itself, any more than other pre -
ventable secondary complications, all persons with SCI should be 
screened for depression, and treatment with psychotherapy and 
psychopharmacotherapy should be initiated if indicated. Most 
people with SCI and depression currently do not receive guide -
line-level treatments for depression.91 Depression can occur at any 
time after SCI, although the prevalence is higher within the first 
few years after SCI. As medical care continues to improve and 
persons survive severe SCI that previously may have been fatal or 
as those with SCI live longer and age with disability, ethical issues 
surrounding the end of life may arise. Living wills and end-of-life 
planning are important. Although euthanasia is not legal in the 
United States, some countries allow it after severe SCI.250
Quality of Life
Quality of life is a concept that is difficult to define but that can 
be described as a determination of individuals’ satisfaction with life 
(e.g., whether individuals are able to do the things they personally 
want to do). Compared with nondisabled people, persons with 
SCI tend to report decreased well-being and a poorer state of 
health; they also score lower on the physical, emotional, and social 
health domains of life.73 A meta-analysis has shown no relationship

between NLI or completeness of SCI and subjective quality of life. 
Some factors are thought to affect quality of life positively, such as 
mobility and ADL independence, emotional support, good overall 
health, self-esteem, absence of depression, physical and social ac -
tivities and integration, being married and employed, having 
completed more years of education, and living at home. In general, 
dissatisfaction with life after SCI seems more related to social dis-
advantages than to physical limitations.
T echnology has enhanced the quality of life for persons with 
disability. The advent of smart home technology can allow an 
individual to open and close doors, turn lights on and off, control 
technology, and much more with using only the voice or a mobile 
phone application. There are also advances in brain-machine in -
terface technology with the goal of allowing people to control 
wheelchairs and other devices with their thoughts.
Late Neurological Decline
New motor or sensory deficits are reported to develop in  
approximately 20% to 30% of persons with chronic SCI. 187 The 
most common etiology is entrapment of a peripheral nerve, espe-
cially of the median nerve at the carpal tunnel or of the ulnar 
nerve at the elbow. Further damage of the spinal cord can be 
caused by posttraumatic syringomyelia (PTS), also called post -
traumatic cystic myelopathy, by tethering or by compression of 
the spinal cord.
Although it is common to find MRI evidence of a cyst within 
the spinal cord at the level of the injury, only 5% of all people with 
SCI develop PTS. It becomes a problem when the cyst expands 
longitudinally and damages the cord, causing clinical symptoms 
such as pain, sensory loss, weakness, altered muscle tone, and a 
variety of autonomic symptoms.187 The exact cause of PTS is un-
known, but it might be related to obstruction of the normal flow 
of CSF because of scarring or canal narrowing. This leads to abnor-
mal increases in CSF pressure during coughing and straining, ulti-
mately causing a longitudinal dissection of the cord.187
The diagnosis of PTS is confirmed by MRI or CT myelogra -
phy with delayed images. The clinical symptoms of PTS and 
their progression vary greatly. If no neurological decline is noted 
on regular follow-up examinations, the treatment can be symp -
tomatic. Activity restrictions are usually advisable, especially 
avoiding strenuous exercise that could raise venous and CSF 
pressure. When PTS is associated with progressive neurological 
decline or intractable pain, surgical treatment is usually indicated 
to reduce the size of the syrinx or to prevent its expansion. Surgi -
cal approaches include placement of a shunt in the syrinx to 
drain the fluid into the peritoneal, pleural, or subarachnoid 
spaces; marsupialization of the syrinx; and duraplasty. 187 When 
treatment is successful, strength may improve, and pain and 
spasticity may diminish.
T ethering of the spinal cord as a result of meningeal or arach-
noid scar formations can occur after SCI and prevent normal 
rostrocaudal sliding of the cord within the spinal canal on mo -
tion. T ethering of the cord in the cervical spine can generate 
enough cord traction with flexion of the neck to cause cord or 
brainstem displacement and neurological symptoms such as 
weakness, sensory deficits, and pain. If this occurs, surgical unte -
thering should be considered.
Late compression of the spinal cord or nerve roots can occur for 
multiple reasons and cause neurological decline. Common causes 
include progressive spondylosis, spinal stenosis, intervertebral disc 
herniations, and posttraumatic changes. Proper imaging studies are 
needed for diagnosis. Surgical intervention can be implemented if 
there is rapid neurological loss or severe, intractable pain.
Secondary Conditions
Pulmonary System
Pulmonary complications, including atelectasis, pneumonia, re -
spiratory failure, pleural complications, and PE, are the leading 
causes of death for persons with SCI in all years after SCI.222 In a 
large sample from the Model SCI Care Systems and Shriner’s 
Hospitals, PE accounted for 37% of all deaths during the first 
year after SCI and 21% of the deaths beyond the first year. 69
The incidence of respiratory failure after acute tetraplegia is as 
high as 74%, with up to 95% of patients with injury levels above 
C5 and AIS A status requiring mechanical ventilation at least 
temporarily.55
Children with SCI have a higher mortality and risk of com -
plications due pulmonary issues as compared with adults for 
several reasons, including increased restrictive lung disease 
caused by the ubiquity of scoliosis, impaired respiratory devel -
opment in infants and young children, and increased risk of 
sleep-disordered breathing in infants and young children with 
cervical injuries. 217
Anatomy and Mechanics of the Pulmonary System. SCI can 
lead to alterations in lung, chest wall, and airway mechanics. The 
degree of respiratory dysfunction after SCI is strongly correlated 
with the NLI and degree of motor impairment. The pulmonary 
function profile of persons with chronic tetraplegia and high 
paraplegia reveals decreased lung volumes and lower thoracic wall 
compliance as a result of the restriction caused by respiratory 
muscle weakness as well as airway hyperreactivity. Specifically, 
spirometric and lung volume studies in persons with tetraplegia 
and high levels of paraplegia demonstrate a reduction of VC, 
total lung capacity, expiratory reserve volume, and inspiratory 
capacity, along with an increase in residual volume (RV), and 
little or no change in functional residual capacity. 211 The dia -
phragm, innervated by anterior horn cells located in the C3–C5 
segments, is the major primary muscle of inspiration; however, it 
is important to note that individuals with lower NLI are also at 
increased risk of respiratory complications related to the numer -
ous other muscles that are important to normal respiratory  
function ( Fig. 50.19 ). The sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 
muscles, innervated by the spinal accessory nerve and the C2–C4 
and C1–C4 roots, respectively, are accessory muscles of inspira -
tion and can be necessary to allow adequate ventilation in per -
sons with higher level SCI. Use of a simple bedside spirometer to 
measure VC can give a quantitative measure of respiratory mus -
cle strength that is equivalent to doing a manual muscle test of 
an extremity muscle. Inspiratory resistive training and aerobic 
exercise training at a high level (70%–80% of maximal heart 
rate) in persons with tetraplegia have been shown to improve the 
strength and endurance of a weak diaphragm and to improve 
lung function.
The VC of persons with tetraplegia or high paraplegia is pos -
turally dependent, being up to 15% lower in the upright position 
than in the supine position. When persons with paralyzed ab -
dominal muscles are in a sitting position, the effect of gravity on 
the abdominal contents leads to an increased RV . Use of an ab -
dominal binder in the sitting position helps reverse this effect by 
holding the abdominal contents within the abdominal cavity 
supporting the diaphragm and thus enabling a more efficient  
diaphragmatic resting position.

1131Chapter 50  Spinal Cord Injury
The airway hyperreactivity seen in persons with SCI is respon-
sive to inhaled bronchodilators and is thought to be caused by 
unopposed cholinergic tone.211
Persons with NLI of C2 or above with a complete SCI usually 
have no diaphragmatic function and require mechanical ventila -
tion or diaphragmatic or phrenic pacing. Persons with C3 AIS A 
or B SCI have severe diaphragmatic weakness and commonly re -
quire mechanical ventilation at least temporarily. Persons with C4 
AIS A or B SCI often also have severe diaphragmatic weakness 
and may require mechanical ventilation at least temporarily. Per -
sons with C5–C8 AIS A or B SCI are usually able to maintain 
independent breathing, but because of the loss of innervation to 
the intercostal and abdominal muscles, they remain at high risk 
for pulmonary complications. This risk for pulmonary complica -
tions is also present for those with thoracic-level AIS A or B SCI, 
albeit to a lesser degree depending on the segmental extent of the 
loss of innervation.
Management of Pulmonary Complications. Atelectasis is the  
most common respiratory complication in people with SCI and 
can predispose to pneumonia, pleural effusion, and empyema. 
Pneumonia commonly occurs in areas of atelectasis. Pleural effu -
sions often develop in close proximity to areas of atelectasis. It is 
thought that the areas of lung that collapse pull the parietal pleu -
ral away from the visceral pleura, leaving an empty space that 
consequently then fills with a fluid, causing an effusion. T reat -
ment of atelectasis includes lung expansion, secretion mobiliza -
tion, and secretion clearance. Although increasing tidal volume 
(TV) has been shown to improve outcomes in individuals with 
SCI receiving mechanical ventilation in patients with subacute 
tetraplegia, high TV ventilation in the acute, intensive care course 
may result in ventilator-acquired pneumonia or prolonged reli-
ance on mechanical ventilation.
120 In the subacute phase of SCI, 
such as when a person is in acute inpatient rehabilitation, TV may 
be increased more freely. If an individual is receiving mechanical

ventilation, a gradual increase in TV to a target of greater than  
20 mL/kg of ideal body weight (IBW) has been shown to be ef -
fective in decreasing atelectasis as compared with maintaining the 
TV at less than 20 mL/kg IBW .55 A prospective randomized trial 
comparing 20 mL/kg IBW TV and 10 mL/kg IBW TV during 
weaning in patients with subacute tetraplegia did not show any 
differences in adverse events in either group, indicating that the 
use of higher TV can be safe in persons with SCI without  
evidence of acute respiratory distress syndrome .
94 Intermittent 
positive-pressure breathing, bilevel positive airway pressure, or 
continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) devices can all be 
used with or without tracheostomy tubes to help with lung expan-
sion and to prevent or treat atelectasis. Secretion mobilization 
techniques include postural drainage and chest percussion or vi -
bration. Postural drainage uses gravity to assist in the drainage of 
accumulated secretion from specific lung areas. Chest percussion, 
optimally performed in conjunction with postural drainage, can 
be performed with a cupped hand, with a mechanical vibrator, by 
donning a vibrating vest, or by lying in a vibrating bed. Secretion 
clearance techniques include suctioning, manually assisted cough, 
use of a mechanical insufflator-exsufflator, and bronchoscopy. The 
insufflator-exsufflator supplies positive pressure to the airway, fol-
lowed immediately by negative pressure, either through the 
mouth or a tracheostomy tube. This rapid pressure change in -
duces a high expiratory flow similar to that of a cough. It is less 
traumatic than suctioning. Bronchoscopy is usually reserved for 
persistent atelectasis or lung collapse.
Medications are useful adjuncts in treating and preventing at -
electasis. Bronchodilators reduce the airway hyperreactivity and 
inflammation that contribute to atelectasis formation and sputum 
production; they also stimulate the secretion of surfactant. Use of 
a beta
2-adenergic medication has been shown to improve expira -
tory pressures, which can lead to a more effective cough. Mucolyt-
ics such as guaifenesin can be given orally; others, such as  
acetylcysteine, can be given via a nebulizer. Adequate hydration 
will help to thin pulmonary secretions.
Indications for the initiation of mechanical ventilation in -
clude physical signs of respiratory distress (cyanosis, accessory 
muscle use, tachypnea, tachycardia, diaphoresis, altered mental 
status, hypotension, hypertension), hypercarbia (partial pressure 
of carbon dioxide in arterial blood [Paco
2] . 50 mm Hg), hy-
poxia (partial pressure of oxygen in arterial blood [Pao 2]  
, 50 mm Hg) unresponsive to oxygen therapy, a falling VC 
(, 15 mL/kg IBW), and/or an inability to handle secretions. If 
intubation is expected to last beyond 5 days, a tracheostomy 
should be performed.
The mode of mechanical ventilation used conventionally in a 
rehabilitation setting has been assist-control or controlled manda-
tory ventilation during rest. Weaning then occurs as progressive 
ventilator-free breathing (PVFB), starting with a trial of as little as 
2 minutes/day with the individual completely disconnected from 
the ventilator. Oxygen is given via a tracheostomy mask or  
T-piece. The time away from the ventilator is gradually increased 
as tolerated in single or multiple trials per day, depending on the 
length of each trial. It should be borne in mind that after lengthy 
mechanical ventilation, the diaphragm muscle becomes atrophied 
and deconditioned, requiring a lengthy period of reconditioning 
before ventilator-free breathing is achieved. PVFB is useful for 
persons who are not expected to fully wean as well, giving them 
some confidence and endurance to remain off the ventilator for a 
short time if an unforeseen problem arises with their ventilator 
setup.
